data.history: The research provides information about two distinct Al Zamel entities with no confirmed family connection. The first involves Doaa Al Zamel, a Syrian refugee born in Daraa in 1995, whose father worked as a barber before fleeing the Syrian civil war in 2012 when his shop was destroyed. Her family moved to Egypt, where conditions deteriorated under Abdel Fattah el-Sisi's leadership, prompting Doaa and her fiancé Bassem to attempt migration to Europe in 2014. During this journey, Doaa survived the Malta migrant shipwreck that killed approximately 500 people, eventually being resettled in Sweden. The second reference appears in relation to Al Sadd Sports Club in Qatar, where 'Al Zamel' is mentioned as a nickname meaning 'The Leader' used for the club, though this may be a mistranslation of 'Al Zaeem.' No verified genealogical, business, or tribal connections are established in the research between these references.
data.description: Research shows Syrian refugee family with father working as barber, forced to flee civil war. No confirmed GCC family genealogy or tribal connections established in available sources.
data.originStory: Doaa Al Zamel's family originated from Daraa, Syria, two hours from Damascus, where her father worked as a barber before the Syrian civil war forced their displacement.
data.modernStatus: No confirmed GCC business or political status established in research.

migrationPath.description: Family fled Syrian civil war after father's barber shop was destroyed

data.history: The Al Sabah family has ruled Kuwait since the 18th century, establishing themselves as the ruling dynasty of what became the modern State of Kuwait. The family traces its lineage through several key branches, with power traditionally alternating between the descendants of Sheikh Salem al-Mubarak al-Sabah and Sheikh Ahmad al-Jaber al-Mubarak al-Sabah. The current Emir, Sheikh Mishal al-Ahmad al-Jaber al-Sabah (born September 27, 1940), represents the 17th ruler from the Al Sabah family and the seventh since Kuwait's independence from the United Kingdom. His father, Sheikh Ahmad al-Jaber al-Mubarak al-Sabah, was a former ruler of Kuwait, and Sheikh Mishal is the younger half-brother of three previous Emirs: Sheikh Jaber al-Ahmad (1977-2006), Sheikh Sabah al-Ahmad (2006-2020), and Sheikh Nawaf al-Ahmad (2020-2023). The family has maintained political control through constitutional monarchy, with succession typically following established protocols within the two main family branches.
data.description: The Al Sabah family is the ruling dynasty of Kuwait, having governed the emirate since the 18th century through a constitutional monarchy system. The family alternates power between two main branches descended from Sheikh Salem al-Mubarak and Sheikh Ahmad al-Jaber al-Mubarak al-Sabah.
data.modernStatus: Currently the ruling family of Kuwait under Emir Sheikh Mishal al-Ahmad al-Jaber al-Sabah since December 16, 2023. The family maintains political control through constitutional monarchy, with members holding key government positions including Crown Prince Sheikh Sabah Khalid al-Hamad al-Sabah (appointed June 2, 2024) and various ministerial roles. The family has suspended parliament and some constitutional provisions as of May 2024.

data.history: Al Zuhayk is one of the two major Alid families of Karbala, claiming descent from Ibrahim al-Asghar bin Musa al-Kadhim, the seventh Shia Imam. They are named after their patriarch Yahya Zuhayk, great-great-great-grandson of Abdullah al-Ha'iri, the naqib of the Talibids who moved to Karbala in the early 11th century. By 1317, when the Ilkhanate state was disintegrating and left the Euphrates region without stable government, Karbala split into two factions - Al Faiz and Al Zuhayk - leading to a feud that lasted nearly half a century. Al Zuhayk believed the niqaba (supervision of Sayyids) of Karbala belonged to them because they previously held the niqaba of the Talibids of Iraq, while Al Faiz claimed it as the older residing family. When the Al Muhanna tribe invaded Karbala in 1355 to exploit the chaos, both Al Faiz and Al Zuhayk united to expel the invaders, forming an alliance where Abu al-Qasim Muhammad, son of Yahya Zuhayk, became naqib while Al Faiz took the governorship and shrine custodianship. The two families intermarried to strengthen their alliance. Today, Al Zuhayk is known by the families of Al Thabit, Al Daraj (later Al Naqib), and Al Wahab, with Al Eshaiker and Al Jolokhan families as their cousins who also took the Zuhayk name.
data.description: Al Zuhayk is a major Alid family of Karbala descended from Ibrahim al-Asghar bin Musa al-Kadhim, named after patriarch Yahya Zuhayk. They historically competed with Al Faiz family for religious and political control of Karbala before forming an alliance in the 14th century.
data.originStory: Al Zuhayk descends from Ibrahim al-Asghar bin Musa al-Kadhim, the seventh Shia Imam. Their specific patriarch is Yahya Zuhayk, who was the great-great-great-grandson of Abdullah al-Ha'iri, the naqib of the Talibids who moved to Karbala in the early 11th century. All descendants of al-Asghar had united under their cousin Yahya Zuhayk's name by the early 14th century.
data.modernStatus: Al Zuhayk continues as one of the major Alid families in Karbala, organized into several branch families including Al Thabit, Al Daraj (Al Naqib), Al Wahab, Al Eshaiker, and Al Jolokhan. Members have held various religious, political, and business positions into the modern era.

data.history: The Al Mahafdha family is documented through Yousef al-Mahafdha (also spelled Almuhfdha), a prominent Bahraini human rights advisor and researcher. He served as a board member of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights (BCHR) and became acting head of the organization after the arrest of president Nabeel Rajab. During the 2011 Bahraini uprising, al-Mahafdha participated in pro-democracy protests, including the March 12, 2011 march on the royal palace. His activism led to sustained harassment by security forces, with 25 plainclothes officers searching his family home and threatening nightly returns until he surrendered to police. He faced multiple arrests at checkpoints in 2012 and was detained in November 2012 in Diraz for participating in what authorities called an 'illegal gathering.' His case gained international attention when Amnesty International designated him a prisoner of conscience, stating he was 'detained solely for exercising his rights to freedom of expression, association and assembly.' The family name appears in Arabic as سيد يوسف المحافظة (Sayed Yusuf Almuhfdha), indicating possible sayyid lineage, though no specific tribal origins are documented in the available research.
data.description: A Bahraini family known through human rights activist Yousef al-Mahafdha, who served as acting head of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights and was designated a prisoner of conscience during the 2011 uprising.
data.modernStatus: The family is known for human rights activism, with Yousef al-Mahafdha serving as acting president of the banned Bahrain Centre for Human Rights and continuing advocacy work despite government persecution and multiple arrests.

data.history: The Al Tuwaijri family emerged as influential advisors and administrators in Saudi Arabia during the 20th century. Abdulaziz bin Abd al-Muhsin al-Tuwaijri (1912–2007) was one of the editors of Al Qassim, a weekly nationalist paper launched in December 1959. He first met Prince Abdullah in the 1960s when the latter was made head of the National Guard, which was then an extremely small and disorganized force. Al-Tuwaijri advised the Prince to transform this nominal position into real power, and over the years they created a modern and sophisticated force with top-class housing, medical care and education for its members. This transformed the National Guard into an independent empire loyal to Abdullah, with many attributing its success to al-Tuwaijri's hard work and foresight. He became Prince Abdullah's close confidant and special advisor, known to have Ba'athist sympathies and friendships with Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser. Following King Faisal's murder in 1975, al-Tuwaijri continued as Abdullah's top aide through his appointments as second deputy prime minister, Crown Prince, and regent in the mid-1990s. He became the most powerful non-royal citizen in the country until retiring in 2005. His son Khaled succeeded him as chief of the Crown Prince's Court in early 2005, later becoming chief of the Royal Court under King Abdullah in October 2005. Khaled consolidated power by 2011, bringing all government business under his purview as chief of the Cabinet Court and secretary-general of the Allegiance Council, effectively becoming the most powerful non-royal in the kingdom until his dismissal by King Salman in 2015 and subsequent arrest in the 2017 corruption crackdown.
data.description: The Al Tuwaijri family served as powerful non-royal advisors and administrators in Saudi Arabia, with Abdulaziz al-Tuwaijri transforming the National Guard alongside Prince Abdullah, and his son Khaled becoming the kingdom's most influential non-royal official before falling from grace in 2015.
data.modernStatus: The family's political influence ended with Khaled al-Tuwaijri's dismissal by King Salman in 2015 and his arrest in November 2017 during the anti-corruption crackdown, though he was freed in January 2018.

data.history: The Al Muqrin family is the ancestral lineage of the House of Saud, the ruling dynasty of Saudi Arabia. The earliest recorded ancestor was Mani' bin Rabiah Al-Muraydi, who settled in Diriyah in 1446–1447 with his clan, the Mrudah. The Mrudah are believed to be descended from the Banu Hanifa branch of the Rabi'ah tribal confederation. Over time, the Al Muqrin became the ruling family among the Mrudah in Diriyah, which prospered along the banks of Wadi Hanifa and became an important Najdi settlement. The family name comes from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725. His son Muhammad bin Saud Al Muqrin (1687–1765) founded the First Saudi State in 1727 through his historic alliance with religious reformer Muhammad bin Abd al-Wahhab in 1744/1745. This partnership established the political and religious foundations that would eventually evolve into the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The Al Muqrin lineage directly led to the House of Saud, named after Saud bin Muhammad Al Muqrin, with Muhammad bin Saud's descendants continuing to rule through various Saudi states until the present day under King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud.
data.description: The Al Muqrin are the ancestral family of the Saudi royal House of Saud, tracing back to 15th-century settlers in Diriyah who became the founding dynasty of Saudi Arabia. Their descendants have ruled various Saudi states from 1727 to the present day.
data.originStory: Mani' bin Rabiah Al-Muraydi settled in Diriyah in 1446–1447 with his clan, the Mrudah, after being invited by a relative named Ibn Dir who ruled villages and estates near modern-day Riyadh. Ibn Dir gave Mani two estates called al-Mulaybeed and Ghusayba, and the family renamed the region Al Diriyah after their benefactor.
data.tribalOrigin: Banu Hanifa branch of the Rabi'ah tribal confederation
data.modernStatus: The Al Muqrin lineage continues as the House of Saud, the ruling royal family of Saudi Arabia. Current King Salman bin Abdulaziz represents this bloodline, with the family controlling vast oil wealth estimated at $1.4 trillion and wielding absolute political power over the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
data.nameEtymology: The Al Muqrin name comes from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725. The family was later renamed Al Saud after his son Saud bin Muhammad Al Muqrin.

data.history: The Al Aiban family has emerged as a prominent family in Saudi Arabia's modern political and security establishment. The family's rise to prominence began with Muhammad Al-Aiban, who served as the first head of the Saudi General Intelligence Presidency, establishing the family's credentials in the kingdom's security apparatus. His son Musaad bin Mohammed Al Aiban has continued this legacy, serving as National Security adviser, Minister of State, and member of the Council of Ministers since 1995, making him one of the oldest members of the Council. The family has maintained close ties to multiple Saudi sovereigns, with Musaad serving as a trusted envoy to King Fahd and King Abdullah. Another son, Bandar Al-Aiban, served as head of the Saudi Human Rights Commission, demonstrating the family's involvement across different government sectors. The Al Aiban family represents the educated technocratic elite of modern Saudi Arabia, with members holding advanced degrees from institutions like King Saud University and Harvard University, and serving in key advisory roles within the Royal Court and various government ministries.
data.description: A prominent Saudi family known for their leadership roles in intelligence, national security, and government administration. The family has produced key figures in Saudi Arabia's modern political establishment.
data.modernStatus: Currently holds significant positions in Saudi government with Musaad bin Mohammed Al Aiban serving as National Security adviser, Minister of State, and Council of Ministers member since 1995, participating in high-level diplomatic summits and serving on both the Council of Political and Security Affairs and Council of Economic and Development Affairs

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Mheiri family specifically. The most prominent contemporary figure is Mariam Al Mheiri, who serves as the UAE's Minister of State for Food and Water Security and is listed among nine female Cabinet members in the UAE government. Additionally, filmmaker Fadel Al Mheiri represents the family in the cultural sector, having graduated from the American University of Sharjah and establishing himself in the Emirates' film industry. The research does not provide information about the family's historical origins, tribal connections, or migration patterns. The Al Mheiri name appears in contemporary UAE governmental and cultural contexts, indicating their integration into modern Emirati society and their participation in the country's political and cultural development.

data.history: The Al Jaber family referenced in this research appears to be part of the Al-Sabah ruling dynasty of Kuwait, specifically the branch descended from Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah. Sheikh Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, born in Kuwait City on 29 June 1926, was the third son of Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah and became the 13th ruler in the Al-Sabah family dynasty. He served as Kuwait's ruler from 31 December 1977 until his death on 15 January 2006, having previously held key government positions including minister of finance and economy from 1962 to 1965, and later prime minister. His rule spanned critical periods in Kuwaiti history including the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), the Iraqi invasion and Gulf War (1990-1991), and the 2003 US invasion of Iraq, during which he maintained Kuwait's sovereignty and strategic alliances.
data.description: The Al Jaber name in this context refers to a branch of Kuwait's ruling Al-Sabah dynasty. Sheikh Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah served as Kuwait's ruler for nearly three decades until 2006.
data.modernStatus: This branch of the Al-Sabah ruling family held Kuwait's highest political office, with Sheikh Jaber serving as the country's ruler until 2006. The family maintained significant political influence and leadership within Kuwait's constitutional monarchy system.

data.history: The Al Jassim family appears to be a Saudi Arabian family primarily associated with the Eastern Province and Al-Ahsa region, based on available research about footballer Tayseer Al-Jassim. Tayseer Al-Jassim was born on July 25, 1984, in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia, specifically in Al-Ahsa Governorate in the village of Bani Ma'an. He is the brother of former player Misfer Al-Jassim, suggesting the family has produced multiple notable athletes. The family appears to have roots in the village of Bani Ma'an in Al-Ahsa, which is located in Saudi Arabia's oil-rich Eastern Province. Beyond this athletic connection, the research does not provide comprehensive information about the broader Al Jassim family history, origins, or tribal affiliations.
data.description: The Al Jassim family is a Saudi Arabian family from the Eastern Province, particularly associated with Al-Ahsa and the village of Bani Ma'an, known for producing notable footballers.
data.modernStatus: The family has gained recognition primarily through sports, with Tayseer Al-Jassim being a prominent Saudi footballer who played for Al-Ahli and the Saudi national team, and served as vice president of Al-Ahli's board of directors.

data.history: The Rashidi dynasty, also known as Al Rashid or the House of Rashid, was a historic Arabian ruling house that controlled the Emirate of Ha'il from 1836 to 1921. The dynasty was founded by Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid, who established control over Ha'il in northern Najd after leading a revolt against the existing ruler Muhammad bin Ali al-Jaafar al-Shammari. Ha'il derived its wealth from being positioned on the Hajj pilgrimage route to Mecca and serving as an important commercial center. The Rashidi emirs became the most formidable enemies of the House of Saud, rulers of the Emirate of Nejd, and cooperated closely with the Ottoman Empire throughout much of their rule. In 1890, Al Rashid occupied Riyadh and defeated the Saudi tribes, forcing them into exile in Bahrain, Qatar, and finally Kuwait. However, the dynasty suffered from succession disputes and internal violence, with six Rashidi leaders dying violently in the late nineteenth century. The lack of an accepted succession rule led to conflicts over whether leadership should pass horizontally to brothers or vertically to sons. During the early 20th century, as Ottoman influence waned and Ibn Saud returned from exile in 1904 to retake Riyadh, the Rashidi power declined. By 1921, Ha'il was captured by Abdulaziz Al Saud, ending Rashidi rule. Many family members went into voluntary exile, primarily to Iraq, Pakistan, Oman, Kuwait, and the UAE, with only a handful remaining in Saudi Arabia by the 1990s.
data.description: The Rashidi dynasty was a historic Arabian ruling house that controlled the Emirate of Ha'il from 1836-1921, serving as the primary rivals to the House of Saud before being conquered by Ibn Saud.
data.originStory: The dynasty was founded by Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid, who came to power after receiving support from Imam Faisal bin Turki bin Abdullah Al Saud to officially take over the Emirate of Ha'il from Muhammad bin Ali al-Jaafar al-Shammari. Abdullah led a revolt together with his brother prince Ubayd Al Rashid against the existing ruler, with support from their cousin Zamil Bin Sabhan from Al Sabhan Family.
data.modernStatus: Most family members went into exile after 1921, primarily to Iraq, Pakistan, Oman, Kuwait, and the UAE. By the 1990s, only a handful remained inside Saudi Arabia.
data.nameEtymology: The Rashidi dynasty derived their name from their forebear Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid, the first emir who founded the dynasty.

migrationPath.description: Voluntary exile of family members after the fall of Ha'il to Ibn Saud

data.history: The Kooheji family is a prominent Bahraini family with Persian origins, as evidenced by their name which derives from Kuhij (also spelled Kooheji). Based on the research, they have established themselves as a significant business and political family in modern Bahrain. The family has been involved in construction and contracting through Kooheji Contractors, which built major commercial projects like the Fakhro Tower in Manama. Politically, the family gained prominence when Hamad Al Kooheji was elected to represent the First District in Muharraq Governorate in the Council of Representatives in 2018, despite facing a targeted social media campaign against his family during the election. The family has also produced notable figures in media and sports, including TV presenter Ameera Al-Kooheji and mixed martial artist Hamza Kooheji who trained with the KHK MMA fight team alongside international fighters like Khabib Nurmagomedov.
data.description: A Bahraini family of Persian origin involved in construction, politics, media, and sports, with notable presence in Muharraq Governorate and connections to major business projects in Manama.
data.originStory: The family traces its origins to Kuhij, indicating Persian ancestry, though the specific details of their migration to Bahrain are not provided in the available research.
data.modernStatus: The Kooheji family maintains significant influence in Bahrain through multiple sectors: construction via Kooheji Contractors, political representation through Hamad Al Kooheji in the Council of Representatives, media presence through TV personality Ameera Al-Kooheji, and sports connections through Hamza Kooheji's involvement with KHK MMA.

data.history: Al Tajir is a branch of the ancient Al Faiz family of Karbala, tracing their lineage to Ibrahim al-Mujab, the 9th-century grandson of the seventh Shi'ite Imam, Musa al-Kadhim. The Al Faiz family has occupied and held custodianship of Karbala's holy sites since 861 AD, making them the oldest Alid family in the city. Al Tajir specifically branched off from Ali 'al-Tajir' al-Faizi, who was named 'al-Tajir' (the merchant) due to his frequent travels to East Asia for trade. The family owns farms known as Umm al-Sudan in mahalat Al Faiz (now known as al-Hayabi), which Hassan al-Tajir endowed to his descendants in 1680. Throughout their history, the Al Tajir family has served in the holy Husayn and Abbas shrines in Karbala, maintaining their religious and custodial duties. The family is part of the larger Al Faiz confederation that includes Al Tumah, Al Nasrallah, Al Dhiya al-Din, Al Awj, and Al Sayyid Amin families, all descendants of Muhammad Abu al-Faiz.
data.description: Al Tajir is a merchant family branch of the ancient Al Faiz clan of Karbala, descended from 9th-century Shi'ite Imam lineage. They are known for their historical role as custodians of holy shrines and their trade connections to East Asia.
data.originStory: Al Tajir descended from Ali 'al-Tajir' al-Faizi, who was part of the Al Faiz family lineage tracing back to Ibrahim al-Mujab bin Muhammad al-Abid bin Musa al-Kadhim. Ibrahim al-Mujab was the first known Alid to settle in Karbala in 861 AD after al-Mutawakkil was killed and his son al-Muntasir provided protection to the Alids.
data.tribalOrigin: Al Faiz (Alid family descended from Musa al-Kadhim)
data.modernStatus: The family continues to maintain their historical properties and religious connections in Karbala, with members serving in various capacities related to the holy shrines.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Tajir means 'the merchant' in Arabic, referring to Ali al-Tajir's frequent travels to East Asia for trade purposes.

data.history: The Al Busaidi dynasty (House of Al Bu Said) was founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi in 1744 and represents one of the oldest surviving royal dynasties in Arabia. The dynasty traces its roots to the Azd tribe through a patrilineal ancestor, al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally hailed from Yemen and migrated north after the destruction of the Marib Dam, later establishing themselves as a leading force in Muslim conquests through the celebrated general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. Ahmad bin Said rose to power as governor of Sohar when Persian forces attacked the town in 1737. After enduring a nine-month siege, he negotiated terms with the Persians, but by 1747 he had massacred the remaining Persian troops at a banquet in Barka fort. He was elected Imam on November 20, 1744, marking the last time Oman was occupied by foreign parties and beginning a unified state that continues today. The dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Said bin Sultan (Said the Great, 1804-1856), who expanded Omani territory to include Zanzibar, parts of Southeast Africa, southern Iran, and Balochistan. He established Zanzibar as his second capital in 1832 and developed extensive trade networks. Upon Said's death in 1856, the empire was divided into two sultanates: Muscat and Oman under Thuwaini bin Said, and Zanzibar under Majid bin Said. The Zanzibar line ended with the 1964 revolution, while the Omani line continues today under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said, who succeeded Sultan Qaboos bin Said in 2020.
data.description: The Al Busaidi (Al Bu Said) dynasty is the current ruling royal family of Oman, founded in 1744 by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi. Tracing their origins to the Azd tribe of Yemen, they established one of the oldest surviving royal dynasties in Arabia and ruled the Omani Empire at its zenith in the 19th century.
data.originStory: The Al Busaidi trace their ancestry to the Azd tribe through al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally came from Yemen, migrating north after the destruction of the Marib Dam. The family name derives from Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), a celebrated general of the Azd tribe who served in the Umayyad Caliphate. Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi was born in 1694 in Adam, Oman, the son of Sa'id bin Ahmad Al-Busaidi, and came from a small Hinawi tribe from the interior of Oman.
data.modernStatus: The Al Busaidi dynasty currently rules the Sultanate of Oman under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said, who ascended to power in 2020. Crown Prince Theyazin bin Haitham was appointed as heir apparent in 2021. Family members hold various government positions, including Foreign Minister Badr bin Hamad Al Busaidi. The royal family uses the title Sayyid/Sayyida, with male line descendants of Sultan Said bin Sultan and Sultan Turki bin Said entitled to use His/Her Highness.
data.nameEtymology: Al Bu Said derives from Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah, known as Abu Said, a celebrated Umayyad general and progenitor of the Busaid tribe from the Azd confederation.

data.history: The Al Nabhani (Nabhani dynasty) were rulers of Oman from 1154 until 1624, when the Yaruba dynasty took power. The Bani Nabhan were one of the Arab tribes of the interior with an ascribed Azdi origin. After the collapse of the Makramid dynasty, the Buyids appointed the Bani Nabhan as the governors of Sohar. The Nabhanids retained their power under the transition to Seljuk rule, and emerged as sovereign rulers after the waning of the Seljuk state in 1154. Muhammed al-Fallah of the Banu Nabhan emerged as a powerful leader in 1151 and had taken control by 1154, living until 1176. The Nabhans ruled as muluk (kings) and made their capital at Bahla, controlling the valuable frankincense trade route from Dhofar via Sohar to the Yabrin oasis, and then north to Bahrain, Baghdad and Damascus. The Banu Nabhan were dominant over the other tribes until the end of the 15th century, with records of personal visits by Nabhani rulers to Ethiopia, Zanzibar, the Lamu Archipelago of what is now Kenya, and Persia. The al-Nabhani dynasty of Pate Island in the Lamu Archipelago claimed descent from the Omani dynasty. The dynasty declined from the 15th century onwards, with the last Nabhani ruler being removed in 1624 when Nasir bin Murshid of the Ya'Aruba took control. However, the Nabahinah retained power at the beginning of the Ya'rubi state, treating Jabal al-akhdar as an emirate and becoming the tamimah of the Banu Riyam. They survived as princes of the Jabal al-Akhdar until they were defeated in the war of Jabal Akhdar in 1956, when Sheikh Suleiman bin Himyar Al-Nabhani fled to Saudi Arabia, returning to Oman in 1996 where he died in 1998.
data.description: The Al Nabhani were the ruling dynasty of Oman from 1154-1624, descended from the Arab Azdi tribes, who controlled the interior from their capital at Bahla and dominated the lucrative frankincense trade. After losing power to the Ya'Aruba, they survived as princes of Jabal al-Akhdar until 1956.
data.originStory: The Bani Nabhan were one of the Arab tribes of the interior with an ascribed Azdi origin. After the collapse of the Makramid dynasty, the Buyids appointed the Bani Nabhan as the governors of Sohar, and they retained their power under Seljuk rule before emerging as sovereign rulers in 1154.
data.modernStatus: Most descendants remain living in Muscat, the capital of Oman, following the return of the last prominent leader Suleiman bin Himyar Al-Nabhani from Saudi exile in 1996 until his death in 1998.

data.history: The Al Ismaili family name derives from Ismailism, a branch of Shia Islam that emerged from a succession crisis in early Islamic history. Ismailis get their name from their acceptance of Imam Isma'il ibn Jafar as the appointed spiritual successor to Ja'far al-Sadiq, differing from Twelver Shia who accept Musa al-Kazim. The Ismaili community developed through several historical phases, including the Fatimid Caliphate (910-1169 CE), the Alamut period under Hassan-i Sabbah, and various splits that created different branches. Today, Ismailis are found across Central Asia, Afghanistan, Iran, Pakistan, Yemen, Lebanon, Malaysia, Syria, India, Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Iraq, Kuwait, East Africa, and other regions, having emigrated globally in recent years. The largest segment are the Nizari Ismailis, who recognize the Aga Khan as their hereditary Imam, with the current Imam being Aga Khan V (Shah Rahim al-Hussaini) who succeeded his father in February 2025.
data.description: Al Ismaili refers to followers of Ismailism, a branch of Shia Islam that accepts Imam Isma'il ibn Jafar as the sixth Imam. The community is globally distributed with significant populations in the Gulf region among other areas.
data.originStory: The Ismaili tradition traces its origins to the succession crisis following the death of Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq in 765 CE, when followers of his son Isma'il ibn Jafar separated from other Shia groups who followed Musa al-Kazim.
data.modernStatus: The global Ismaili community is led by the Aga Khan V, with the Aga Khan Development Network operating various development programs internationally. Ismailis are found in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states as part of the broader global community.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Ismaili' derives from Imam Isma'il ibn Jafar, whom this branch of Shia Islam accepts as the appointed spiritual successor to Ja'far al-Sadiq.

data.history: The Al Mutairi (المطيري) family name appears in the research primarily through Abdullah Al-Mutairi, a professional footballer born July 1, 1986, who was born into an Al Mutairi family living in Mecca. He spent his entire career representing various clubs in Saudi Arabia as a defending midfielder, scoring only two goals before retiring in 2020 due to injury. The research also mentions a Kuwaiti extremist preacher named Hakem al-Mutairi in connection with discussions about regime change in Saudi Arabia with Muammar Gaddafi and Persian Gulf power brokers. Beyond these specific individuals, the research provides no information about the broader Al Mutairi family's origins, tribal connections, migration patterns, or historical development in the Gulf region.

data.history: The Al Khulaifi family appears in the research as prominent figures in Qatar and the broader Gulf region, though their specific origins are not detailed in the provided sources. The family has produced notable academics, with Ibrahim Al-Khulaifi serving as a researcher who conducted studies on educational television programming in Kuwait in the 1980s. The family has been involved in various professional fields including academia, with members teaching at Qatar University, and business activities including banking and sports administration. Sultan Khlaifa al-Khulaifi emerged as a human rights activist and blogger, serving as Secretary-General of the Swiss-based Alkarama Foundation until 2010, when he became involved in advocacy work that led to his detention in Qatar in 2011 for expressing political views critical of the government. The family name appears in various contexts across the Gulf states, suggesting a distributed presence in the region.

data.history:
The Al Abdullah family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf and Arab world, with the most prominent being the Hashemite branch ruling Jordan. King Abdullah II of Jordan, born in 1962, is the current monarch who ascended to the throne in 1999 following his father King Hussein's death. The Jordanian Al Abdullah lineage traces back to the Hashemites, who ruled Mecca for over 700 years from the 10th century until 1925, and have ruled Jordan since 1921. According to family tradition, they are the 41st-generation descendants of the Prophet Muhammad through his daughter Fatimah and Ali, the fourth Rashidun caliph. The family's modern political prominence began when Abdullah I founded modern Jordan, with Abdullah II being his namesake and great-grandson.

In Saudi Arabia, a separate Al Abdullah lineage existed through King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1924-2015), who ruled from 2005 until his death. Born as the tenth son of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz, his mother Fahda bint Asi Al Shuraim was from the Al Rashid dynasty, longtime rivals of the Al Saud. He served as Crown Prince from 1982 and became de facto ruler in 1995 when King Fahd suffered a stroke. His reign was marked by economic reforms, interfaith dialogue initiatives, and maintaining close ties with Western allies.

In Kuwait, Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah represents the Al Abdullah branch within the ruling Al Sabah family. Born in 1952 and educated in finance at the University of Illinois, he has held various ministerial positions including oil minister (2009-2011) and was appointed Prime Minister-designate in April 2024. These various Al Abdullah lineages demonstrate the family name's presence across different Gulf ruling dynasties, each with distinct origins and roles in their respective nations' political and economic development.
data.description: Al Abdullah represents multiple prominent lineages across the Arab world, most notably the Hashemite rulers of Jordan who claim descent from Prophet Muhammad, the Saudi royal family branch, and members of Kuwait's ruling Al Sabah dynasty. Each lineage has played significant roles in modern Gulf and Arab political history.
data.originStory: The Jordanian Al Abdullah branch traces their origins to the Hashemites, who according to family tradition are 41st-generation descendants of Prophet Muhammad through his daughter Fatimah and her husband Ali. The Hashemites ruled Mecca for over 700 years from the 10th century until the House of Saud conquered it in 1925, before establishing their rule in Jordan in 1921.
data.modernStatus: The family holds key positions across multiple Gulf states: King Abdullah II rules Jordan as a constitutional monarch with executive powers, the late King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia (d. 2015) was one of the wealthiest monarchs globally, and Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Sabah serves as Kuwait's Prime Minister since May 2024, having previously held ministerial positions including oil minister.
data.nameEtymology: Abdullah II of Jordan was named after his paternal great-grandfather, Abdullah I, who founded modern Jordan

data.history: The Al Khodari family is a Saudi Arabian family with documented presence in the Kingdom. Based on available research, the family has produced notable figures in sports and the arts. Maan Sultan Al-Khodari, born December 13, 1991, is a professional footballer who plays as a defensive midfielder and represented Saudi Arabia in international competitions including the 2011 FIFA U-20 World Cup. The family name also appears in Syrian cultural circles through Ribal Al-Khodari, born November 18, 1988, a Syrian singer, composer, and choir conductor who studied at the Higher Institute of Music in Damascus before being forced to leave Syria during the civil war. He relocated first to Jordan where he established the Ammon Choir (later renamed Aman Choir), and subsequently moved to Germany where he continues his musical career.

data.history:
The Al Othman family name derives from the Arabic name 'Uthman,' which has deep historical significance in Islamic history as the name of the third Rashidun Caliph, Uthman ibn Affan (c. 573-656 CE). Caliph Uthman was a wealthy merchant from the Banu Umayya clan who became one of the earliest converts to Islam and married two daughters of Prophet Muhammad, earning him the title 'Possessor of Two Lights.' He is credited with commissioning the standardized compilation of the Quran around 650 CE and oversaw significant territorial expansion of the Islamic state.

The research reveals several individuals with variants of the Al Othman name from different regions and time periods. Othman Ahmed Othman Al Omairah (also transliterated as Othman Ahmad Othman al-Ghamdi, 1979-2015) was a Saudi citizen held at Guantanamo Bay whose identification was inconsistent across official documents, with some U.S. reports indicating Yemeni citizenship. Mohammed Uthman al-Mirghani al-Khatim (1793-1852) was born in Mecca to the noble Mirghani family, claimed descent from Prophet Muhammad, and founded the Khatmiyya Sufi order with followers across Egypt, Sudan, Eritrea, Somalia, and Ethiopia.

The name variations and geographical spread suggest the Al Othman designation represents multiple family lines across the Arabian Peninsula and broader Islamic world, united primarily by their connection to the revered name of Caliph Uthman rather than a single ancestral lineage. The historical prominence of the name in Islamic civilization has led to its adoption by various families throughout the Muslim world.
data.description: Al Othman is a patronymic family name derived from Uthman, the third Rashidun Caliph, found among various family lines across the Arabian Peninsula and Islamic world. The name carries significant religious and historical prestige in Islamic culture.
data.originStory: The name Al Othman derives from Uthman ibn Affan, the third Caliph of Islam (c. 573-656 CE), who was a wealthy merchant from Mecca's Banu Umayya clan and early convert to Islam. Known as 'Possessor of Two Lights' for marrying two daughters of Prophet Muhammad, he standardized the Quran compilation and expanded Islamic territories before his assassination in 656 CE.
data.modernStatus: The research indicates various Al Othman family members across different regions and professions, but does not provide comprehensive information about current unified business or political status.
data.nameEtymology: Al Othman derives from the Arabic name 'Uthman' (عثمان), meaning 'baby bustard' or referring to the bird species. The name gained immense prestige through Uthman ibn Affan, the third Rashidun Caliph of Islam.

data.history: Based on the available research, Al Salam appears as a family name in multiple distinct contexts across different regions and time periods. The most prominently documented is the Lebanese Al Salam family, represented by Nawaf Salam and his relatives, who constitute a prominent Sunni Muslim political dynasty from Beirut. Nawaf Salam's grandfather, Salim Salam, led the 'Beirut Reform Movement' calling for Ottoman Empire decentralization and modernization, serving as deputy of Beirut to the Ottoman parliament in 1912. The family has maintained significant political influence in Lebanon, with Saeb Salam serving four times as Prime Minister between 1952-1973, Tammam Salam serving as Prime Minister 2014-2016, and Nawaf Salam currently serving as the 53rd Prime Minister since February 2025. The research also references other unrelated entities using the Al Salam name, including a criminal organization in Europe and references to national symbols, but these appear to be separate from the Lebanese political family and do not represent a unified genealogical lineage.
data.description: Al Salam appears to represent multiple distinct families and entities across different regions, most notably a prominent Lebanese Sunni political dynasty from Beirut with over a century of political leadership.
data.modernStatus: The Lebanese Al Salam family maintains significant political influence, with Nawaf Salam currently serving as Lebanon's Prime Minister since February 2025, following a family tradition of high-level political service spanning multiple generations.
data.nameEtymology: Al Salam literally means 'The Peace' in Arabic

data.history: The research provided does not contain specific information about the Al Luhaidan family's origins, migration history, or early development. The only direct reference to the Al Luhaidan family appears in connection with Saleh Al-Luhaidan, who is identified as a Saudi scholar and member of the Council of Senior Scholars. According to Wikipedia sources, Saleh Al-Luhaidan is noted as belonging to the Subay' tribe, which is described as an Arabian tribe of North Arabian (Adnanite) stock that traces its lineage to the ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir (also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a). The Subay' tribe came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period and comprised both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections at the turn of the 20th century. The sedentary members of Subay' have resided in central Arabia for centuries, with families belonging to this tribe found in practically every town in the region, making up a significant portion of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. In terms of modern political involvement, Saleh Al-Luhaidan held significant religious authority as both a scholar and judicial figure, serving as head of the Supreme Judicial Council until his removal by King Abdullah in 2009.
data.description: The Al Luhaidan family appears to be connected to the Subay' tribe of central Arabia, with notable representation in Saudi Arabia's religious and judicial establishment through figures like Saleh Al-Luhaidan.
data.modernStatus: The family has notable representation in Saudi Arabia's religious establishment, with Saleh Al-Luhaidan having served as a member of the Council of Senior Scholars and head of the Supreme Judicial Council until 2009.

data.history: The Al Otaibi family belongs to the 'Utaybah tribe, one of the most pre-eminent tribes of the Najd region in Saudi Arabia. The tribe has a significant historical presence, with members participating in major events including the Battle of Sabilla during the Ikhwan uprising against King Abdulaziz. Juhayman al-Otaybi was born in al-Sajir, Al-Qassim Province, a settlement established by King Abdulaziz to house Ikhwan Bedouin tribesmen who had fought for him, populated by members of his tribe. Many of Juhayman's relatives, including his father and grandfather Sultan bin Bajad al-Otaybi, participated in the Battle of Sabilla during the Ikhwan uprising against King Abdulaziz. The family has produced notable figures across different eras, from religious dissidents to modern women's rights activists and former royalty through marriage.
data.description: Al Otaibi is a prominent Saudi Arabian family from the 'Utaybah tribe, one of the most pre-eminent tribes of the Najd region, with members spanning from historical Ikhwan fighters to modern activists and public figures.
data.originStory: The Al Otaibi family originates from the 'Utaybah tribe, one of the most pre-eminent tribes of the Najd region in Saudi Arabia, with historical ties to the Ikhwan Bedouin movement.
data.modernStatus: The family includes contemporary figures such as women's rights activists, with some members achieving international recognition for human rights advocacy, while others have connections to Saudi royalty through marriage.

data.history: The al-Shehri family originates from the Asir Province in southwestern Saudi Arabia, specifically from the impoverished region near the Yemeni border. The family name denotes membership in Bani Shehr, an Arabic tribal confederation. The family was based in Khamis Mushait, with Wail and Waleed al-Shehri growing up in the Um Saraar neighborhood. Their father, Mohammed Ali Asgley Al Shehri, worked as a car dealer, while several brothers joined the Saudi military and an uncle served as a major in the army and director of logistics. The family built and attended Al-Seqley Mosque as their local place of worship. The family strictly adhered to Wahhabism, forbidding modern elements like satellite television, Internet, music, and contact with non-mahram females. The al-Shehri family had connections to the bin Laden family, with the father being friends with Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden, Osama bin Laden's father. Two family members, Wail and Waleed al-Shehri, became notorious as participants in the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks, serving as hijackers on American Airlines Flight 11.
data.description: The al-Shehri family is a Saudi Arabian family from the Asir Province near the Yemeni border, members of the Bani Shehr tribal confederation. The family became internationally known due to two brothers who participated in the September 11 attacks.
data.originStory: The al-Shehri family originates from the Asir Province in southwestern Saudi Arabia, an impoverished region along the Yemeni border. They are members of Bani Shehr, as indicated by their surname which denotes tribal affiliation.
data.modernStatus: The family remains in Khamis Mushait, Saudi Arabia, with members working in various sectors including the military and commerce. The family gained international notoriety following the September 11 attacks.
data.nameEtymology: al-Shehri denotes membership in Bani Shehr, when followed by a sun letter, the 'l' in 'al' assimilates to the initial consonant of the following noun, resulting in a doubled consonant

data.history: The Al Attas family traces its origins to the Ba'Alawi sayyids of Hadramaut, with genealogical records extending over a thousand years through Hadrami Arab sayyid lineage. The family established itself across the Malay world through historical migration patterns from Hadramaut to Southeast Asia. Notable family members include Syed Muhammad Naquib al-Attas (1931-2026), a prominent Malaysian Muslim philosopher born in Bogor, Java, who became a leading Islamic scholar and was awarded the title of Royal Professor in Malaysia. Another branch includes Hamida al-Attas (née Alia Ghanem), who came from a Syrian Alawite family of citrus farmers and married Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden in 1956, later marrying Mohammed al-Attas, a Hadhrami administrator in the Bin Laden business empire. The family has maintained scholarly and administrative traditions across multiple generations in both Southeast Asia and the Arab world.
data.description: The Al Attas family is a Hadrami Arab sayyid lineage with over a thousand years of documented genealogy, established across Southeast Asia and the Arab world, known for producing prominent Islamic scholars and administrators.
data.originStory: The Al Attas lineage descends from the Ba'Alawi sayyids of Hadramaut, tracing genealogical connections through traditional Hadrami Arab scholarly families that migrated to establish communities in the Malay world and other regions.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains scholarly and academic prominence, with members holding positions as Royal Professors, philosophers, and administrators across Malaysia, Indonesia, and other regions where Hadrami communities established themselves.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Attas translates to 'the sneezer' in Arabic, with origins traced back through Islamic lineage to a man named Alawi bin Muhammad, who sneezed during a gathering, prompting the name to be bestowed upon his descendants as a marker of divine blessing.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Hadrami Arab families to Southeast Asia

data.history: The Al Bukhari family name derives from the city of Bukhara in present-day Uzbekistan, most famously associated with the 9th-century Islamic scholar Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari (810-870 CE), compiler of Sahih al-Bukhari, one of the most important hadith collections in Sunni Islam. Al-Bukhari was of Persian descent, with his great-grandfather Al-Mughirah settling in Bukhara after converting to Islam under the governor Yaman al-Ju'fi, adopting the nisba 'al-Ju'fi.' His earliest known ancestor was Bardizbah, a Zoroastrian Magi. The family's scholarly tradition continued through various branches that migrated across the Islamic world. Sayyid Jalaluddin Surkh-Posh Bukhari (1190-1295 CE) was a Sufi saint who migrated from Bukhara to the Indian subcontinent, settling first in Sindh and later in Uch, where he founded the Jalali Sufi order and converted numerous tribes to Islam. His descendants, known as Naqvi al-Bukhari, spread across Punjab, Sindh, and northern India. In modern times, the Bukhari name appears among various communities, including those in the Gulf region who may trace ancestry to Central Asian scholars or Sufi traditions, though specific migration patterns to the GCC are not detailed in available sources.
data.description: Al Bukhari families trace their name to the ancient city of Bukhara in Central Asia, most notably through the lineage of the great Islamic scholar Imam al-Bukhari and Sufi saints who migrated throughout the Islamic world.
data.originStory: The Al Bukhari name originates from Bukhara, an ancient center of Islamic learning in present-day Uzbekistan. The most famous bearer was Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari, whose great-grandfather Al-Mughirah converted to Islam in Bukhara and adopted the local tribal affiliation al-Ju'fi through his patron Yaman al-Ju'fi, the city's governor.
data.modernStatus: Al Bukhari descendants are found across the Islamic world, particularly in South Asia where they maintain scholarly and Sufi traditions. Some members have achieved prominence in various fields, though specific modern business or political activities in the Gulf are not documented in available sources.

folkLegends.title: Chenghiz Khan's Fire Test
folkLegends.story: According to legend, Jalaluddin Surkh-Posh was imprisoned by Chenghiz Khan while traveling to India. When he refused to abandon his faith, Chenghiz attempted to have him burned alive, but the fire did not harm him. Impressed, Chenghiz released him and married his daughter to the saint.

folkLegends.title: The Restored Sight
folkLegends.story: When young Muhammad al-Bukhari lost his sight, his mother prayed fervently and saw the prophet Ibrahim in a dream who told her that Allah had restored her son's sight due to her tears and prayers. The boy awoke with his vision restored.

data.nameEtymology: Al Bukhari means 'from Bukhara,' derived from the ancient Central Asian city that was a major center of Islamic scholarship and trade along the Silk Road.

data.history: The Al Dawadmi family appears to be connected to the region of Dawadmi in Saudi Arabia, located approximately 400 kilometers from Riyadh. Based on the research, Dawadmi served as a location where various Otaibah tribe settlements were established during the Ikhwan movement in the early 20th century. Multiple settlements were founded in proximity to Dawadmi, including Al Hafira (48 km away), Arwa (99 km south), Nafi (90 km north), Aseela (95 km northeast), Arja'a (28 km north), and Musada (10 km north). The area became significant during the period of tribal settlement from nomadic life into permanent communities, particularly during the reign of King Abdul Aziz Ibn Saud. The region witnessed various administrative and political developments as these settlements were governed by tribal leaders who participated in the unification of Saudi Arabia. However, the research does not provide direct information about the Al Dawadmi family's specific origins, founding members, or detailed family history beyond the geographical connection to the Dawadmi region.
data.description: A family name associated with the Dawadmi region of Saudi Arabia, an area that became a center for Otaibah tribal settlements during the early 20th century Ikhwan movement.

The Al Dumat family name appears to be connected to the historic location of Dumat al-Jandal, an oasis market town in northern Arabia that played a significant role in early Islamic history. During the time of Prophet Muhammad, Khalid ibn al-Walid captured Dumat al-Jandal in 630 CE, imposing a heavy penalty on its inhabitants and forcing its Kindite chief Ukaydir ibn Abd al-Malik al-Sakuni to sign a capitulation treaty. The town was strategically located connecting Medina to the regions of Bahrayn and Oman in eastern Arabia. Later, during the Ridda wars around 634 CE, Khalid was again involved in military operations at Dumat al-Jandal, where combined Muslim forces defeated the defenders who were backed by Byzantine-confederate tribes including the Ghassanids, Tanukhids, Salihids, Bahra and Banu Kalb. The town's conquest was significant enough that it was mentioned multiple times in early Islamic historical records, suggesting families may have taken their name from this important location.
data.description: A family name likely derived from the historic oasis town of Dumat al-Jandal in northern Arabia, which was a strategically important location during the early Islamic period and the Ridda wars.
data.nameEtymology: Likely derived from Dumat al-Jandal, an oasis market town in northern Arabia that was strategically important during the early Islamic conquests

data.history: The Al Maktoum family, descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, established their rule over Dubai through a lineage that can be traced through the research to Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum Al Maktoum, father of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum (1912-1990). Sheikh Rashid transformed Dubai from a small settlement around Dubai Creek into a modern port city and regional commercial hub, implementing widespread reforms and infrastructure development despite limited initial revenues. His leadership included the dredging of Dubai Creek (1958-1959), establishment of Dubai Police (1956), Dubai Municipality (1957), and construction of Maktoum Bridge (1963). The discovery of oil in 1966 at the Fateh field enabled massive expansion, including the construction of Port Rashid (opened 1972) and later Jebel Ali Port (1983), which became the world's largest man-made harbour. Sheikh Rashid was instrumental in the formation of the United Arab Emirates, meeting with Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan in February 1968 at Argoub Al Sedirah to establish the foundation of the union through what became known as 'the only nation in the world founded on a handshake.' His son Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum succeeded his brother Maktoum as ruler in 2006, continuing the family's political dominance while overseeing Dubai's transformation into a global city through projects like the Palm Islands, Burj Al Arab, and Burj Khalifa, alongside establishing major enterprises including Emirates airline, DP World, and the Jumeirah Group.
data.description: The Al Maktoum family are the ruling family of Dubai, descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, who transformed Dubai from a small Creek settlement into a global city and major commercial hub. They have held continuous political power in Dubai while building major business enterprises including Emirates airline and Dubai World.
data.originStory: The research indicates the Al Maktoum family are descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, with Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum serving as the tribal leader of Al-Falasi, but no specific origin story or founding narrative is provided in the research materials.
data.modernStatus: The Al Maktoum family maintains absolute rule over Dubai through Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, who serves as ruler of Dubai, Vice President and Prime Minister of the UAE. They control major business holdings through Dubai World and Dubai Holding, including Emirates airline, DP World, Nakheel Properties, and Jumeirah Group, with significant real estate ownership under Dubai law that grants the ruling family ownership of all undeveloped land.

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Fouzan family specifically. The name Fouzan appears to derive from Arabic origins meaning 'successful' or 'victorious,' with the root word 'fouz' representing success. The suffix 'an' in Arabic represents the dual tense, making 'Fouzan' literally mean 'dual success.' The term appears in the Quran multiple times, including in Surah Fath ('The Victory'), where it appears as 'Fouzan-Azeema' meaning 'Great Success' in the context of achieving Paradise. However, the research does not provide specific historical information about the Al Fouzan family's origins, settlement patterns, or development as a family group.
data.description: An Arabic family name meaning 'dual success,' derived from Quranic terminology, though specific family history is not documented in the available research.
data.nameEtymology: Derived from Arabic 'fouz' meaning success, with suffix 'an' representing dual tense, literally meaning 'dual success.' The term appears in the Quran in Surah Fath as 'Fouzan-Azeema' meaning 'Great Success' or 'The Highest Achievement' in the context of eternal Paradise.

data.history: The research provided does not contain information about a family specifically called 'Al Sultan.' The Wikipedia entries discuss three different individuals with 'Sultan' in their names from different ruling families: Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan (1955-2019) from the UAE's Al Nahyan family, Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said (born 1955) who is the current Sultan of Oman from the Al Said dynasty, and Prince Sultan bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1925-2011) from Saudi Arabia's Al Saud family. These are distinct royal lineages rather than branches of a single 'Al Sultan' family. Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed was a member of the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi, serving as Deputy Prime Minister of the UAE and dying in 2019. Sultan Haitham bin Tariq represents the Al Said dynasty of Oman, ascending to the throne in 2020 after his cousin Sultan Qaboos. Prince Sultan bin Abdulaziz was from the Al Saud family, serving as Saudi Arabia's long-time Defense Minister and Crown Prince until his death in 2011.
data.description: The research does not identify a specific family called 'Al Sultan' but rather discusses three separate royal figures with 'Sultan' in their names from different Gulf ruling dynasties.
data.modernStatus: No unified 'Al Sultan' family identified in the research

data.history: The Al Mousa family research reveals multiple distinct lineages across different regions. In Saudi Arabia, the family produced notable footballers including Ahmed Al-Mousa (born 1981) who played for Al-Ittihad and Al-Wahda, leading the latter to third place before defeating Al-Shabab in playoffs. Ahmed represented Saudi Arabia in the 2007 AFC Asian Cup and 19th Arabian Gulf Cup, and is brother to fellow players Motaz (Al Ahli) and Kamil (Al Wahda). Another Saudi branch includes Mansour Al-Mousa (born 1970), who played for Al-Najma and represented Saudi Arabia in the 1994 World Cup and later played for Al-Nassr in the 2000 Club World Cup in Brazil. In Kuwait, the name appears among the Ajam community, with references to families of Iranian descent including those involved in business ventures like Jasem M. al-Mousa Trading, owned by a former Minister of Public Works. The research also documents an Iranian political figure, Mousa Khiabani (1947-1982), who was a leading member of the People's Mojahedin of Iran (MEK) and commander of its armed wing from 1979-1982.
data.description: Al Mousa appears as multiple distinct family lineages across the Gulf region, including Saudi Arabian football families, Kuwaiti merchant families of possible Iranian origin, and Iranian political figures, with no confirmed connection between the various branches.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Mousa families are involved in professional sports in Saudi Arabia, with multiple family members playing for major clubs and representing the national team. In Kuwait, the family has business interests including companies connected to former government ministers.
data.nameEtymology: The name Mousa corresponds to the Arabic name Mūsā (موسی), meaning 'Moses' in reference to the biblical prophet.

data.history: The Al-Kharafi family established themselves as one of Kuwait's most prominent business and political dynasties through their founding of M.A. Kharafi & Sons, which became a major conglomerate dominating construction, telecommunications, and investment sectors in Kuwait. The family patriarch Mohammed Abdulmohsen al-Kharafi founded the company, which served as the foundation for the family's wealth and influence. During the reign of Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah, the Kharafi business empire held significant sway over multiple sectors of the Kuwaiti economy. The family has produced influential figures across academia, politics, and business, with members serving in high-level government positions including Speaker of the National Assembly and university leadership roles. Their wealth and political connections have made them one of Kuwait's most powerful families, with Forbes recognizing family members among the world's richest individuals.
data.description: A prominent Kuwaiti business and political dynasty founded on the M.A. Kharafi & Sons conglomerate, producing influential leaders in politics, academia, and business. The family has held significant economic and political power in modern Kuwait.
data.modernStatus: Major business conglomerate M.A. Kharafi & Sons dominates construction, telecommunications, and investment sectors in Kuwait. Family members have held high political offices including Speaker of National Assembly, and leadership positions in academia including Kuwait University presidency.

data.history: The Al Fares family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Arab world with no confirmed common ancestry. In Jordan, the Al Fares political family includes Abdul Raouf Al Fares, who served as a Jordanian Parliament member from 1954 until his death in 1984, and his son Tahsin Al-Fares, also a Parliament member in 1985. In Syria, several prominent Al Fares figures emerged: Fares al-Khoury (1877-1962) became a foundational figure of modern Syrian politics, serving as Prime Minister twice and helping establish the United Nations in 1945. Nawaf al-Fares, a veteran Ba'ath Party official who served as governor of multiple Syrian provinces and ambassador to Iraq, defected from Assad's government in July 2012 during the Syrian Civil War. The Lebanese Al Fares branch includes entertainers like Myriam Fares, while Swedish-Lebanese actor Fares Fares represents another lineage entirely. These various Al Fares families appear to be separate entities sharing only a common surname, with origins ranging from Christian communities in Syria to Sunni tribal backgrounds in eastern Syria.
data.description: Al Fares represents multiple unrelated Arab families sharing a common surname, with notable political figures in Jordan and Syria, entertainers in Lebanon, and diaspora communities in Europe.
data.modernStatus: Multiple Al Fares families maintain prominence across different Arab countries: political leadership in Jordan and Syria, entertainment industry success in Lebanon, and international diplomatic careers

data.history:
The Al Qadhi family research reveals three distinct branches with different origins and backgrounds. The most documented branch originates from the Pakistani scholar Yasir Qadhi (born 1975), whose father Mazhar Kazi was a doctor from Karachi, Pakistan. The family's roots trace back to Uttar Pradesh, India, with Yasir's father migrating from Jabalpur to Pakistan during partition. This branch has no connection to GCC genealogy, as they are Pakistani Muhajirs who lived in Saudi Arabia temporarily when Yasir was young, before he pursued Islamic scholarship in the United States.

A separate individual named Ali Mohammad Ali Al Qadi (1988-2023) was a Palestinian from Al Bireh in the West Bank, who became a Hamas commander. He was killed by Israeli forces in October 2023. This represents a completely different Al Qadi lineage with Palestinian origins.

The research also references Mohammad Hadi al-Milani, an Iranian-Iraqi Islamic scholar, but he belonged to the Milani family that emigrated from Medina and settled in Milan, West Azerbaijan in the 14th century, claiming descent from the fourth Shia Imam. This family has no established connection to any Al Qadhi lineage in the research provided.
data.description: Multiple unrelated families bearing the Al Qadhi/Al Qadi surname, including Pakistani Muhajir scholars, Palestinian resistance figures, and Iranian-Iraqi clerics, with no confirmed GCC tribal connections in the available research.
data.modernStatus: The Pakistani branch is represented by Islamic scholars and academics in North America, while other branches have been involved in Palestinian resistance movements or Shia religious scholarship.

migrationPath.description: Mazhar Kazi's migration during India-Pakistan partition
Yasir Qadhi's family moved to Saudi Arabia when he was five years old

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Hamar family appears to be connected to Bahrain's government structure, as evidenced by Nabeel bin Yaqub Al-Hamar serving as Bahrain's Information Minister. In this capacity, Al-Hamar made significant decisions regarding media access and press freedom in the country. On 10 May 2002, Minister Al-Hamar banned Al Jazeera correspondents from reporting from inside Bahrain, citing claims that the station was biased towards Israel and against Bahrain. This action demonstrates the family's involvement in Bahrain's political and media landscape during the early 2000s. After improvements in Bahrain-Qatar relations in 2004, Al Jazeera correspondents were allowed to return to Bahrain, though the ministry again banned them in 2010 over reports on poverty in the country. The Al-Hamar family's role in these media decisions reflects their position within Bahrain's governmental hierarchy and their influence over information policy during a period of regional tensions.
data.description: The Al Hamar family is connected to Bahrain's political establishment, with Nabeel bin Yaqub Al-Hamar serving as the country's Information Minister in the early 2000s. The family has been involved in significant media policy decisions affecting regional press freedom.
data.modernStatus: Connected to Bahrain's government structure, with family members holding ministerial positions in information and media policy

data.history: The Janahi family is part of Bahrain's Ajam community, comprising Bahraini citizens of Iranian descent who have settled in the Gulf state over multiple generations. Based on available research, members of the Janahi family appear to be part of the broader Iranian migration waves to Bahrain that occurred primarily between the 19th and 20th centuries, particularly from southern Iranian provinces including Fars, Bushehr, and Hormozgan. The family has established itself as part of Bahrain's diverse ethnic landscape, which includes various Iranian groups such as Achomis, Lurs, Baloch, and Persian speakers who migrated for economic opportunities and to escape political upheaval in Iran. Contemporary members of the Janahi family have achieved prominence in Bahrain's business and political spheres, indicating successful integration into Bahraini society while maintaining cultural connections to their Iranian heritage. The family represents the complex identity dynamics of Bahrain's Ajam community, where individuals navigate between Persian cultural heritage and Bahraini Arab national identity.
data.description: The Janahi family belongs to Bahrain's Ajam community of Iranian descent, with members achieving prominence in business and politics. They represent the successful integration of Iranian families into Bahraini society while maintaining cultural heritage connections.
data.modernStatus: The Janahi family has achieved significant prominence in modern Bahrain across business and political sectors. Notable contemporary figures include Mohammed Hassan Janahi, who serves as a member of parliament and has publicly affirmed Bahrain's Arab identity in political discourse. The business sector includes prominent figures like Sonya Janahi, who serves as an ILO Governing Body Member and Bahrain Chamber Board Member from an Achomi Sunni background, Khaled Janahi as Chairman of Vision 3 and Member of Bahrain Economic Development Board, Neyla Janahi as a Bahrain TV presenter, and Linda Janahi as Chief of Corporate Services at Bahrain EDB. The family's diverse professional achievements span media, international business development, and political representation.

data.history: The Bucheery family is part of the Ajam community of Bahrain, representing Iranian Bahrainis who trace their origins to various regions of Iran. Based on the research, Iranian migration to Bahrain occurred in multiple waves, with the most documented period being between the 19th and 20th centuries (1801-2000 CE). The first significant modern wave occurred around 1860, with families migrating from Bushehr, Bandar Abbas, Bandar Langeh, Mohammerah (Khorramshahr), and Lorestan. In 1860, specifically, the Bushehri family migrated to Bahrain, establishing one of the documented Iranian family presences on the island. A second major wave occurred during the 1920s-1930s under Reza Shah's consolidation efforts, when many Iranian families left due to increased taxation, enforced Western-style clothing, and cultural suppression policies. The community faced various challenges including the 1904 anti-Persian riots in Manama markets, and later discrimination following the 1979 Iranian Revolution, though many Iranian families had become naturalized Bahraini citizens by this period, particularly after King Hamad Bin Isa granted citizenship to many Ajams in 2002.
data.description: The Bucheery family belongs to the Ajam community of Bahrain, representing Iranian Bahrainis who migrated primarily from southern Iranian provinces between the 19th-20th centuries. They are part of a diverse Iranian diaspora that has significantly influenced Bahraini culture, architecture, and commerce.
data.originStory: Part of the broader Iranian migration to Bahrain, with documented waves beginning around 1860 from regions including Bushehr, Fars province, Hormozgan province, and other southern Iranian areas, driven by economic opportunities and later by political pressures under Reza Shah's modernization policies.
data.modernStatus: Iranian-origin families in Bahrain today are largely integrated into Bahraini society, with many holding citizenship granted in 2002 by King Hamad Bin Isa. The community faces some discrimination and loyalty questioning, with restrictions on Persian language education and cultural expression.

migrationPath.description: Part of first generation Iranian migration including Bushehri family migration to Bahrain

data.history: The Al Jishi family is documented in Bahrain with notable prominence in recent decades, particularly in diplomatic, legal, and human rights sectors. Bahia Jawad Al Jishi emerged as a significant figure, born into a family that would produce influential professionals. She pursued higher education at Cairo University for Arabic literature, followed by an MA in politics and history of the Middle East at SOAS in London, and a doctorate at Boston University. Her career began in 1971 at Bahrain Broadcasting as a newscaster and producer, later working with BBC Arabic from 1975-1978 before returning to Bahrain Broadcasting. She transitioned to government service in 1979 as director of the Directorate of Children, also serving as a UNICEF programme coordinator from 1988-1996. Her political career culminated in 2000 when she became one of the first female members of Bahrain's Consultative Council and helped draft the National Action Charter. Since 2015, she has served as Bahrain's ambassador to Belgium, Luxembourg, and later Denmark, while representing the country at the European Union and NATO. The family also produced legal professionals, with Mohammed al-Jishi serving as a prominent human rights lawyer representing high-profile political activists like Abdulhadi al-Khawaja during the Bahraini uprising period. Additionally, there is a Lebanese-American branch with Zahra Jishi working as a translator of Arabic literature.

data.history: The Abdulrahman family appears to encompass multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf region, with no single unified origin story supported by the available research. The most prominent branch is connected to the Al Thani ruling family of Qatar, where Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani serves as Prime Minister and Foreign Minister since 2016 and 2023 respectively. His lineage traces through Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani, connecting to the broader House of Thani which originated from the Najdi town of Ushaiger and settled in Qatar around the 1720s. Another significant branch appears in Bahrain's political history, where Abdulrahman al-Nuaimi (1943-2011) founded important opposition movements including the Popular Front for the Liberation of Bahrain in the 1970s and later the National Democratic Action Society. The research also reveals other Abdulrahman family members across various fields including Kurdish-Iraqi engineer Basima Abdulrahman, and multiple individuals in sports, politics, and business throughout the Gulf region, though their genealogical connections remain unclear from the available sources.

data.history: Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum was born on 15 July 1949 as the third of four sons of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, ruler of Dubai. The Al Maktoum family is Dubai's ruling family and descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, of which Mohammed is the tribal leader. His mother was Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan, daughter of former ruler of Abu Dhabi Sheikh Hamdan bin Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. Upon Mohammed's return from military training at age 20, his father appointed him as head of the Dubai Police Force and Dubai Defence Force. When the UAE was founded on 2 December 1971, Mohammed became its first minister of defence at age 22. On 3 January 1995, his brother Sheikh Maktoum bin Rashid Al Maktoum appointed Mohammed as crown prince of Dubai. Mohammed became ruler of Dubai on 4 January 2006 upon his brother's death, and was subsequently selected as UAE vice president and prime minister. He has overseen Dubai's transformation into a global city, launching government enterprises including Emirates airline, DP World, and Jumeirah Group, while developing landmark projects like the Palm Islands, Burj Al Arab, and Burj Khalifa.
data.description: The Al Maktoum family are the ruling royal family of Dubai, descendants of the House of Al-Falasi tribe, with Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum serving as current ruler of Dubai, UAE Vice President and Prime Minister since 2006.
data.originStory: The Al Maktoum family is Dubai's ruling family and descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, of which Mohammed is the tribal leader.
data.modernStatus: Ruling family of Dubai with Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum as ruler of Dubai, UAE Vice President, and Prime Minister. Controls major government enterprises including Emirates airline, DP World, Jumeirah Group, Dubai World, and Dubai Holding. Estimated wealth of $14 billion for Mohammed personally, with family assets exceeding $20 billion.

data.history: The research provided focuses exclusively on Gamal Abdel Nasser Hussein (1918-1970), the second president of Egypt, and does not contain information about a GCC family named Nasser. Gamal Abdel Nasser was born in Alexandria, Egypt, to Abdel Nasser Hussein, a postal worker from Beni Mur in Upper Egypt, and Fahima Hammad from the village of Bani Murr near Alexandria. His family had no documented connection to the Arabian Gulf region. The research also mentions his son Khalid Abdel Nasser (1949-2011), who studied at Cairo University and Cambridge University. However, this Egyptian presidential family is distinct from any Gulf merchant or tribal families that might share the surname Nasser. The name Nasser is described as a masculine Arabic given name meaning 'granter of victory,' commonly found throughout the Arabic-speaking world, but the research provides no information about GCC families bearing this name.

data.history: The Jain family name is associated with followers of Jainism, an ancient Indian religion founded in the 6th-5th century BCE. Jainism was established by the 24th Tirthankara Mahavira, who was a contemporary of Buddha, though Jains trace their tradition back through 24 spiritual teachers. The religion emerged from the Śramaṇa movement in Greater Magadha, which rejected Vedic authority. Historically, Jain communities became prominent in commerce, banking, and trade due to their religious principle of ahimsa (non-violence) which discouraged agriculture and warfare. The Sahu Jain family specifically originates from Najibabad in Bijnor district, Uttar Pradesh, and became a major industrial family controlling Bennett, Coleman & Co. Ltd. (The Times Group), which owns The Times of India and other major media properties.
data.description: Jain is a surname associated with followers of Jainism, an ancient Indian religion emphasizing non-violence, with notable modern families like the Sahu Jains who control major Indian media conglomerates.
data.originStory: The name Jain derives from 'jina' meaning 'conqueror' in Sanskrit, referring to those who have conquered their passions and achieved spiritual victory. Adherents are called 'jain' or 'jaina,' meaning 'follower of the conquerors,' replacing the older term 'nirgrantha' (bondless) used for ascetic wanderers.
data.modernStatus: The Sahu Jain family controls Bennett, Coleman & Co. Ltd. (The Times Group), owning The Times of India (world's largest circulated English-language newspaper), The Economic Times, and other major media properties. The family has diversified into education, chemicals, and finance sectors.
data.nameEtymology: Derived from Sanskrit 'ji' meaning 'to conquer,' referring to the spiritual battle against passions and desires. Those who accomplish this conquest are called 'jina' (conqueror), and their followers are 'jain' or 'jaina' (followers of the conquerors).
