data.history: The House of Al Bu Said is the current ruling royal family of Oman, founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi on 20 November 1744. The dynasty traces its roots to the tribes of Azd through a patrilineal ancestor, al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba. Like other Qahtani, the Azd originally hailed from Yemen and migrated north after the destruction of the Marib Dam. With the rise of Islam, the Azd established themselves as a leading force in the Muslim conquests through the celebrated general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. Ahmad bin Said rose to power when he successfully defended Sohar against a Persian fleet attack, holding out for nine months before forcing the Persian forces to leave. He was elected imam on 20 November 1744, marking the last time Oman was occupied by foreign parties and the beginning of a unified state. The dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great,' who extended Oman's borders to include parts of Southeast Africa, Zanzibar, southern Iran, and Gwadar. After his death in 1856, the empire was divided into two separate sultanates: the Sultanate of Zanzibar and the Sultanate of Muscat and Oman. In the modern era, Sultan Qaboos bin Said (1970-2020) transformed Oman from an isolated, underdeveloped country into a modern state through oil revenues, building infrastructure, schools, hospitals, and establishing diplomatic relations globally. He was succeeded in 2020 by his cousin Haitham bin Tariq Al Said.
data.description: The Al Bu Said dynasty is the ruling royal family of Oman since 1744, descended from the Azd tribe of Yemen. They built a maritime empire spanning the Indian Ocean before modernizing Oman in the late 20th century.
data.originStory: The dynasty traces its roots to the Azd tribe through a patrilineal ancestor, al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally hailed from Yemen and migrated north after the destruction of the Marib Dam, later establishing themselves as a leading force in Muslim conquests through their progenitor Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said).
data.tribalOrigin: Azd tribe (branch known as 'Azd of Daba')
data.modernStatus: Currently ruling dynasty of Oman under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said since 2020. The Sultan serves as absolute monarch, prime minister, minister of foreign affairs, minister of defence, and chief of staff of the armed forces.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Khalili family name appears to be connected to historical figures from the Mamluk period in Egypt. During the reign of Sultan Barquq (1382–1399), Jaharkas al-Khalili, who served as Master of the Stables (amir akhur), demolished the Fatimid mausoleum (Turbat az-Za'faraan) to construct a large khan (caravanserai) at the heart of Cairo. This structure became known as Khan al-Khalili, after his name, and eventually gave its name to the entire commercial district that became a major center of foreign trade by the late 15th century. The original khan built by al-Khalili was later demolished and rebuilt in 1511 by Sultan al-Ghuri, becoming known as Khan al-Fisqiya and later as Wikala al-Qutn. However, the research provides no direct connection between this historical Mamluk-era figure and any contemporary Gulf family bearing the Al Khalili name.
data.description: The Al Khalili name is historically associated with Jaharkas al-Khalili, a Mamluk official who built the famous Khan al-Khalili marketplace in Cairo during the 14th century, though no direct connection to contemporary Gulf families is established in the research.
data.nameEtymology: The name Al Khalili derives from Jaharkas al-Khalili, the Mamluk official who constructed Khan al-Khalili in Cairo during the late 14th century

achievements: Established major commercial center in Cairo

data.history: The Al Kindi family traces its lineage to the ancient Arabian tribe of Kinda, descended from the chieftain al-Ash'ath ibn Qays, a contemporary of the Prophet Muhammad. The most famous member of this lineage was Abū Yūsuf Yaʻqūb ibn ʼIsḥāq aṣ-Ṣabbāḥ al-Kindī (c. 801–873 AD), known as 'the father of Arab philosophy' and one of the greatest polymaths of the Islamic Golden Age. Al-Kindi was born in Kufa to an aristocratic family that was among the most prominent of the tribal nobility in early Islamic Kufa, though the family lost much of its power following the revolt of Abd al-Rahman ibn Muhammad ibn al-Ash'ath. His father Ishaq served as governor of Basra, indicating the family's continued political influence despite earlier setbacks. Al-Kindi received his preliminary education in Basra before completing his studies in Baghdad, where he gained the patronage of Abbasid caliphs al-Ma'mun and al-Mu'tasim. Under al-Mu'tasim, al-Kindi was appointed as tutor to the caliph's son and became a prominent figure in the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma), overseeing the translation of Greek texts into Arabic. However, his influence waned under later caliphs, particularly al-Mutawakkil, who persecuted unorthodox Muslims and at one point had al-Kindi beaten and his library temporarily confiscated. The family's historical connection to power is also evidenced through Ja'da bint al-Ash'ath, daughter of al-Ash'ath ibn Qays, who married Hasan ibn Ali, grandson of the Prophet Muhammad.
data.description: Al Kindi is an aristocratic Arabian family descended from the Kinda tribe and the chieftain al-Ash'ath ibn Qays, most famous for producing the renowned 9th-century polymath and philosopher al-Kindi. The family held prominent positions in early Islamic governance before experiencing political decline.
data.originStory: The Al Kindi family descends from the Arabian tribe of Kinda, specifically from the chieftain al-Ash'ath ibn Qays, who was a contemporary of Prophet Muhammad. The family was among the most prominent of the tribal nobility of Kufa in the early Islamic period, holding positions of political authority including governorships.
data.nameEtymology: The name derives from their ancestral tribe Kinda and their most famous ancestor al-Ash'ath ibn Qays al-Kindi

data.history:
The Al Maawali family has demonstrated significant presence in Omani government and military circles in recent decades. Saeed bin Hamoud bin Saeed al Maawali was appointed as Oman's Minister of Transport, Communications and Information Technology on 18 August 2020, having previously worked in the petroleum industry with companies including Petroleum Development Oman, Occidental Petroleum Corporation, and Oman Oil Company. His career trajectory included executive roles at Oman Tank Terminal Company (2014-2018) and CEO of Oman Drydock Company (2018-2020) before his ministerial appointment.

The family has also maintained prominence in Oman's military establishment, with multiple members receiving high military honors. Brigadier General Nasser bin Saleh Al-Maawali and Brigadier General Uday bin Hilal Al Maawali both received the Military Order of Achievement (second class) in 2015, recognizing their distinguished military service of 25 years or more. Additionally, Saeed bin Hamoud Al Maawali received the Civil Order of Oman (second class) in 2024, further demonstrating the family's ongoing role in Omani state institutions.

Politically, the Al Maawali family has representation in Oman's legislative bodies through Khalid Al Mawali, who has served as Chairman of the Consultative Assembly since October 2011, being re-elected to this position four times. This sustained political leadership role indicates the family's established position within Oman's governmental structure and their continued influence in the country's legislative processes.
data.description: A prominent Omani family with significant representation in government, military, and legislative positions, particularly active in ministerial and military leadership roles in contemporary Oman.
data.modernStatus: Currently holds high-ranking positions in Omani government including ministerial appointments, military leadership roles, and legislative positions. Family members have received multiple state honors and maintain active roles in petroleum industry and state enterprises.

data.history: The Al Bulushi family name derives from the Baloch people, an Iranian nomadic pastoral ethnic group originating from the Balochistan region spanning parts of Pakistan, Iran, and Afghanistan. The Baloch have ancient roots, with historians like Agha Mir Nasir Khan Ahmadzai connecting them to the Medes and considering them descendants of ancient Iranian peoples. During the Sasanian era, the Baloch were mentioned in Middle Persian texts as the 'Baločān' who contributed to building cities in the Padishkhwārgar province. The Baloch began major eastward migrations in the late Sasanian period, likely due to unstable conditions in the Caspian region, with waves continuing through the medieval period. By the 9th century, Arab writers described the Baloch as living between Kerman, Khorasan, Sistan, and Makran. The 12th century Seljuk invasion of Kerman stimulated further eastward migration, and by the 13th-14th centuries, Baloch were moving into Sindh, reaching Punjab by the 15th century. The Baloch constitute the largest non-Arab community in Oman at 20% of the population (around 1 million people), where they have been well integrated into political life and hold high-ranking positions, with the first modern Omani army being exclusively Baloch and around 40% of today's Omani Army still consisting of Baloch people.
data.description: Al Bulushi is a family name derived from the Baloch people, an Iranian ethnic group that migrated from ancient Balochistan to various regions including Oman, where they form the largest non-Arab community comprising 20% of the population.
data.originStory: According to Baloch lore, their ancestors originated from Aleppo in Syria, then migrated to the east or southeast of the central Caspian region after conflicts with the Abbasid Caliphate. Based on linguistic analysis of the Balochi language as a Western Iranian language, the original homeland was likely to the east or southeast of the central Caspian region, from where they began migrating eastward in the late Sasanian period.
data.modernStatus: In Oman, the Baloch community is well-integrated with members holding high-ranking government and military positions. They form around 40% of the Omani Army and have played significant roles in the country's development and progress.
data.nameEtymology: The exact origin of 'Baloch' is unclear. Possible derivations include: from 'Balaschik' living in Balasagan between the Caspian Sea and Lake Van; from Sanskrit words 'bal' (strength) and 'och' (high/magnificent); from a Middle Persian term for cockscomb referring to distinctive helmets; or from the Median term 'brza-vačiya' meaning 'loud shouting'.

migrationPath.description: Late Sasanian period eastward migration from central Caspian region to Balochistan
13th-15th century migrations into Sindh and Punjab regions

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Hatmi family appears to be represented across multiple regions and contexts, though no comprehensive genealogical narrative emerges from the available sources. The name 'Al Hatmi' appears in various professional and military contexts in the Arabian Peninsula region, with notable figures serving in positions of authority and expertise. One prominent figure is Air Marshal S. Fauad Masud Hatmi, a senior officer in the Pakistan Air Force who has held various command positions and served as base commander during Operation Swift Retort. Another notable individual is Brigadier General Said bin Abdullah bin Humaid Al Hatmi, who received the Military Order of Oman (Third Class) in 2022, indicating military service within Oman's armed forces. The research also references Qasim Al Hatmi, who served as a referee in international football competitions, including the controversial 2023 SEA Games final between Indonesia and Thailand. Additionally, the name appears in scientific contexts, with 'Al-Hatmi' being associated with mycological research, particularly in the naming of fungal species such as Basidiobolus minor and Basidiobolus omanensis, suggesting possible Omani academic or research connections.
data.description: Al Hatmi appears to be a family name associated with military service, academia, and professional roles across the Arabian Peninsula region, with documented presence in Oman and Pakistan.

data.history: The Al Maani family name appears in various forms across different regions and contexts. According to Wikipedia sources, Maani is a surname found among notable individuals from diverse geographic areas, including Tuvaluan politician Fauoa Maani, Iranian mountaineer Iraj Maani, Jordanian mayor Omar Maani, and New Zealand economics academic Sholeh Maani. The research indicates a potential historical connection to the Ma'n dynasty (alternatively spelled Ma'an), also known as the Ma'nids, who were a family of Druze chiefs of Arab stock based in the rugged Chouf area of southern Mount Lebanon. The most prominent historical figure associated with this lineage was Fakhr al-Din II, also known as Fakhr al-Din ibn Qurqumaz ibn Yunus Ma'n, who served as a Druze emir of Mount Lebanon and was known as Fakhr al-Dinal Ma'ni. However, the research does not provide specific information about how any Al Maani family came to establish themselves in the GCC region, their migration patterns, or their current status in Gulf countries.

data.history: The Al Zedjali family has established a significant presence in Oman's media and diplomatic sectors, though their origins are not documented in the available research. Essa bin Mohammed Al Zedjali founded Muscat Media Group in 1975, launching Times of Oman as a weekly tabloid newspaper on February 23, 1975, which became Oman's oldest English-language newspaper. The company expanded significantly under his leadership, becoming the first publishing house in the GCC countries to develop online editions for its daily newspapers in 2004, and launching multiple publications including the Arabic-language newspaper Al Shabiba in 1993. The family has maintained control of the media empire across generations, with Ahmed Essa Al Zedjali currently serving as CEO of Muscat Media Group. Beyond media, the family has played diplomatic roles, with Essa bin Mohammed Al Zedjali serving as Philippine Honorary Consul General and supporting the establishment of the Philippine School Sultanate of Oman in 1989. The family's influence extends to international recognition, with Tareq bin Mosa la Zedjali receiving Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (Class I: Grand Cordon) in 2017.
data.description: The Al Zedjali family is a prominent Omani family known for founding and leading Muscat Media Group, Oman's largest publishing house, and for diplomatic service including roles as Honorary Consul General.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Busaifi family has produced notable literary and journalistic figures in modern Libya. The most documented member is Mujahid Al-Busaifi, a Libyan writer and journalist who married prominent media executive Huda El-Sarari in 2003. The couple lived in exile in the Netherlands due to their opposition to the Gaddafi regime, later relocating to Qatar. Their story reflects the broader experience of many Libyan intellectual families who were forced into exile during the Gaddafi era for their political opposition and journalistic activities.

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is no direct evidence connecting the Al Toubi family to GCC family genealogy, business history, or tribal lineage. The research focuses on Tawfik Toubi (1922-2011), a Palestinian-born Israeli Arab communist politician from Haifa, whose family name appears to be 'Toubi' rather than 'Al Toubi.' Tawfik Toubi was born to an Arab Orthodox Christian family - his father Elias and mother Alice (née Khoury) - and was educated at Mount Zion School in Jerusalem. He became a prominent figure in Israeli politics, serving over 41 years in the Knesset and helping to found several political movements including the National Liberation League and later Rakah and Hadash parties. His son Elias Toubi studied medicine in Leningrad and became a professor of medicine and director of immunology departments in Israeli hospitals. The research also mentions other figures with similar surnames in different contexts - Faruq al-Qaddumi, a Palestinian politician, and references to various Arab Christian communities - but provides no genealogical connections to establish any unified Al Toubi family history or origins in the Gulf region.
data.description: No verified Al Toubi family information found in the provided research, which focuses primarily on unrelated Palestinian political figures and Arab Christian communities.

data.history: The Al Dhafiri family's most documented member in the research is Mohammed Emwazi (born Muhammad Jassim Abdulkarim Olayan al-Dhafiri), born on 17 August 1988 in Kuwait. The family were Bidoon of Iraqi origin, living in the Taima area of Al Jahra, Kuwait, which was known as a 'slumtown' where stateless people were ghettoized by the Kuwaiti government. They were undocumented, considered stateless and without Kuwaiti citizenship status. The family moved to the United Kingdom in 1994 when Mohammed was six years old, settling in inner west London and moving between several properties in Maida Vale, St John's Wood, and finally Queen's Park. His parents were Jassem and Ghaneyah Emwazi, and Mohammed was the eldest of five children. At some point he became a British citizen, after which he started travelling back to Kuwait, as did his father.
data.description: A family of Iraqi origin who were stateless Bidoon residents in Kuwait before emigrating to the United Kingdom in 1994.
data.originStory: According to the research, the Al Dhafiri family were Bidoon of Iraqi origin who lived in the Taima area of Al Jahra, Kuwait, where they were stateless and without Kuwaiti citizenship.
data.modernStatus: The family's most notable member, Mohammed Emwazi, became internationally known as 'Jihadi John' and was killed in a US drone strike in Syria in 2015.

data.history: The Al Mashaan family appears to have historical connections to the Otaibah tribe's settlement activities in early 20th century Saudi Arabia. Sultan bin Mashaan Abba Al-Ula founded the settlement of Sinam in 1914, leading it alongside Majid bin Jaza Abba Al-Ula. The settlement was strategically located near two valleys - Al Dathma to the north and Wadi Al-Arjan to the south, in the western side of Quweia province. Sultan bin Mashaan played a significant military role in the conquests of King Abdul Aziz, commanding the Sinam Brigade which served as the heart of the battle of Turaba in 1918. By the 21st century, the governance of Sinam had passed to Al Hineedi Omar Aba Abba Al-Ula, indicating continued family leadership of the settlement. The Al Mashaan connection to the broader Abba Al-Ula lineage suggests they were part of the Otaibah tribal structure during the critical period of Saudi state formation and the Ikhwan settlement movement.
data.description: The Al Mashaan family was connected to the founding and leadership of Sinam settlement in 1914, with Sultan bin Mashaan Abba Al-Ula serving as both settlement founder and military commander in King Abdul Aziz's conquests.
data.modernStatus: Historical records show the family's connection to the Sinam settlement, which by the 21st century was governed by Al Hineedi Omar Aba Abba Al-Ula, though current political or business activities are not documented in available sources.

data.history: The Al Shalhoub family has documented origins in Lebanon, with family members tracing their roots to Zahle in the Lebanese mountains. The most prominent documentation comes through American actor Tony Shalhoub, whose paternal grandfather Joseph was from Zahle, Lebanon during the Ottoman Empire period and immigrated to the United States as a child after his parents Milhem and Mariam died during World War I. Joseph Shalhoub eventually settled in Green Bay, Wisconsin, where he worked as a meat peddler and married Helen Seroogy, also of Lebanese descent. The family demonstrates the broader Lebanese Christian diaspora pattern of the early 20th century, with members establishing themselves in business communities abroad. While Tony Shalhoub achieved prominence in American entertainment, his family maintained connections to their Lebanese Christian heritage, with documented ancestry including maternal great-great-grandfather Abdul Naimy, who was reportedly killed during the Hamidian massacres of 1895. The family's integration into American society while preserving Lebanese identity reflects the typical Lebanese immigrant experience of the era.
data.description: Lebanese Christian family with documented roots in Zahle, Lebanon, most prominently known through American actor Tony Shalhoub whose grandfather immigrated to the United States during World War I era.
data.originStory: The Al Shalhoub family originates from Zahle, Lebanon, during the Ottoman Empire period. Tony Shalhoub's grandfather Joseph was from Zahle and immigrated to America as a child after his parents died during World War I, eventually settling in Wisconsin where he worked as a meat peddler.
data.modernStatus: The family achieved prominence through Tony Shalhoub's successful acting career, including multiple Emmy and Tony Awards. The American branch is well-established in Wisconsin with business connections through the maternal Seroogy family candy business.
data.nameEtymology: The research notes a connection to actor Omar Sharif, born Michel Yusef Dimitri Chalhoub, suggesting the name Shalhoub/Chalhoub may have Syrian-Lebanese regional variations, but specific etymology is not provided in the research.

data.history:
The research provides information about several distinct entities named Al Badr, but no single family genealogy. The most prominent historical figure is Muhammad al-Badr (1926-1996), who was the last king and Zaydi Imam of the Mutawakkilite Kingdom of Yemen (North Yemen). Born as the eldest son of Ahmad bin Yahya, al-Badr became Crown Prince in 1955 after helping quell a revolt against his father. He briefly ruled as Imam and King with the title al-Mansur after his father's death on 19 September 1962, but was overthrown just a week later by Abdullah as-Sallal's republican coup on 26 September 1962. Al-Badr escaped to northern Yemen and rallied Zaydi tribes in his support, starting the North Yemen Civil War (1962-1970) with backing from Saudi Arabia against the Egyptian-supported republicans. Despite controlling most of Yemen territorially, Saudi Arabia recognized the republican regime in 1970, leading al-Badr to leave Saudi Arabia for England. He lived quietly in Kent until his death in 1996 and was buried in Medina, Saudi Arabia.

The research also mentions Sayyid Badr bin Hamad Al Busaidi, the current foreign minister of Oman, but he belongs to the Al Busaidi family, not Al Badr. Additionally, there are references to Al-Badr as a militant group in Kashmir and the historical Battle of Badr in Islamic history, but these are unrelated to any Gulf family genealogy. The name 'Al Badr' appears to derive from the Arabic word for 'full moon,' but the research does not establish any unified family lineage connecting these various references.
data.description: The research covers multiple unrelated entities named Al Badr, most notably Muhammad al-Badr, the last Yemeni king who ruled briefly in 1962 before being overthrown and leading royalist forces during the North Yemen Civil War.
data.modernStatus: No current family status identified in the research
data.nameEtymology: Al-Badr derives from Arabic meaning 'the full moon'

data.history:
The Al Saud Al Rashid family designation appears to be a misidentification, as the research clearly shows these are two historically opposing dynasties. The Al Rashid (House of Rashid) were rulers of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from the Shammar tribal confederation, with their capital at Ha'il. They emerged as rulers in the 19th century, with the Al Ali clan initially controlling Ha'il before being replaced by the Al Rashid clan. The Al Rashid became the most formidable enemies of the House of Saud in Najd, cooperating closely with the Ottoman Empire while the Saudis opposed Ottoman rule.

The confusion may stem from marriages between the dynasties, such as when Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid (1898-1920), the tenth Emir of Jabal Shammar, had his widow Fahda bint Asi bin Shuraim Al Shammari later marry Ibn Saud, becoming the mother of King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia. Additionally, Abdullah bin Faisal Al Saud married women from the Rashidi dynasty, including Turayfa bint Ubayd and Noura, daughter of Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid.

The Al Rashid dynasty was ultimately defeated by Ibn Saud's forces, culminating in the Conquest of Ha'il in 1921. Following this defeat, many Shammar and Al Rashid members fled to Syria and Iraq, while some eventually pledged allegiance to Ibn Saud. The historical rivalry between these two houses was fundamental to the political landscape of central Arabia in the 19th and early 20th centuries.
data.description: Al Saud Al Rashid appears to be a misidentification of two historically opposing Arabian dynasties - the House of Saud and the House of Rashid - who were bitter rivals for control of central Arabia until the Al Rashid's defeat in 1921.

data.history: Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy, with ancestral homes traced to areas near Medina in the Hejaz. According to Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes,' Bani Dhafir is a branch of Banu Lam from the Arabs of the Hejaz, coming from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding other tribes and immigration, as they did not have a proper settlement. Historical accounts document conflicts between Al-Dhafeer and the Bani Sakher tribe, where Al-Dhafeer was initially defeated but later regrouped under the leadership of their knight Sultan Ibn Suwit. They followed Bani Sakher north, meeting again in Wadi Azraq (now Azraq) on the Saudi-Jordanian border, where Bani Sakher again defeated Al-Dhafeer, killing Sheikh Sultan Ibn Suwit, who lies buried in that region next to a water ravine named after him (Ghadeer Sultan). In 1165 A.H., the Battle of Rawdat Al-Sabla was fought against Al-Dhafeer from the Banu Khalid, led by Abdullah bin Turki bin Muhammad bin Hussein Al-Hamid, where Al-Dhafeer was ultimately defeated. The 14th-century historian Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari notes that the Anazah were allied with the Al Fadl, along with several other tribes including Al-Dhafeer. Most of the tribe now lives in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors and engineers, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain and Turkey.
data.description: Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe descending from Tayy, originally from the Hejaz region near Medina. They lived in the desert regions of north Najd, north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia and north Kuwait.
data.originStory: The tribe genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy, originating from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn in the Hejaz region. They spread throughout Arabia through raiding other tribes and immigration as the tribe did not have a proper settlement.
data.modernStatus: Most of the tribe now lives in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait in professional positions including professors, politicians, scholars, doctors and engineers, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain and Turkey.

data.history: Faisal Al Duwaish was the Sheikh of the Mutayr tribe and one of the prominent leaders of the Ikhwan movement in the Arabian Peninsula during the early 20th century. The Ikhwan was a Wahhabist-Bedouin tribal army that supported King Abdulaziz Al Saud in his efforts to unify Saudi Arabia between 1910 and 1927. Al Duwaish led the Ikhwan forces in major military campaigns, including the conquest of the Hejaz in the 1920s, and commanded approximately 2,000 men from his base at Al Artawiyah settlement. However, conflicts arose between Al Duwaish and King Abdulaziz after the occupation of the Hejaz, when the king sought to curb incursions outside Arabia and concentrate on building a modern state, which Al Duwaish and other Ikhwan leaders considered sinful departures from Wahhabi principles. In late 1927, Al Duwaish began launching cross-border raids into Mandatory Iraq and Kuwait, challenging Ibn Saud's authority and leading to the Ikhwan revolt. The rebellion escalated through 1928 and culminated in the Battle of Sabilla on March 29, 1929, where Al Duwaish's forces were decisively defeated by Ibn Saud's technologically superior army equipped with machine guns and modern weapons. Al Duwaish fled to Kuwait in October 1929, where he was detained by the British and handed over to Ibn Saud. He died in prison in Riyadh on October 3, 1931, from an apparent heart condition, marking the end of the Ikhwan leadership's challenge to Saudi authority.
data.description: Faisal Al Duwaish was the Sheikh of the Mutayr tribe and a prominent leader of the Ikhwan movement who supported Ibn Saud's unification of Saudi Arabia until leading a rebellion in 1927-1929. He died in Saudi custody in 1931.
data.originStory: The research indicates Al Duwaish was associated with the Mutayr tribe and led the Ikhwan settlement of Al Artawiyah, but does not provide specific details about the family's origins or how they came to prominence within the tribe.
data.modernStatus: The Al Duwaish family's current status is not mentioned in the research. The historical line ended with Faisal Al Duwaish's death in Saudi custody in 1931.

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is no direct information about the Al Khalifa Al Shammari family. The research focuses on the ruling Al Khalifa dynasty of Bahrain, which came to power in 1783 when the Bani Utbah tribal confederation invaded Bahrain and expelled the Persian governor. Ahmed ibn Muhammad ibn Khalifa (Ahmed al-Fateh) became the dynasty's first Hakim of Bahrain. The Al Khalifa family has ruled Bahrain continuously since then, with various rulers including Isa ibn Ali Al Khalifa (1869-1932), Hamad ibn Isa Al Khalifa, and the current King Hamad bin Isa al-Khalifa. However, the research mentions Fahda bint Asi bin Shuraim Al Shammari as one of Ibn Saud's wives, who was the widow of a Rashidi Emir and mother to King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia, indicating some historical connection between Al Shammari lineages and Gulf ruling families.
data.description: No specific information available about the Al Khalifa Al Shammari family in the provided research materials.

data.history:
The Al Hamed family appears to represent multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf region, with documented branches in both the UAE and other Gulf states. In the UAE, Sheikh Ahmed bin Hamed al Hamed (1929-2012) served as the country's first Minister of Information and Tourism, playing a crucial role in establishing the UAE's media infrastructure. He was described as a maternal cousin to Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the UAE's founding president, indicating the family's integration into the Al Nahyan ruling structure of Abu Dhabi. Ahmed bin Hamed al Hamed headed Abu Dhabi's labor department before the UAE's formation and became a founding member of the first Cabinet of Ministers in 1971.

The family's prominence in the UAE stems from their early involvement in the emirate's development during the oil boom of the 1960s and 1970s. Ahmed bin Hamed al Hamed was instrumental in launching Al Ittihad newspaper in 1969, establishing Abu Dhabi television, creating the emirate's first radio station, and organizing archaeological expeditions. He remained in cabinet positions through multiple government formations in the 1970s, serving as Minister of Information and Culture. The family maintained close ties to the Al Nahyan ruling family, with documented marriage connections including Sheikh Hazza bin Zayed Al Nahyan's marriage to Sheikha Mozah bint Mohammed bin Butti Al Hamed.

The research also references an unrelated Al Hamed lineage in Libya, represented by Al-Sadek Hamed Al-Shuwehdy, an aeronautical engineer who was executed in 1984 for opposing Gaddafi's regime. However, no documented connection exists between the Libyan Al-Shuwehdy branch and the UAE Al Hamed family, suggesting these are distinct family lines sharing similar nomenclature.
data.description: The Al Hamed family includes prominent UAE political figures, notably Sheikh Ahmed bin Hamed al Hamed, the country's first Information Minister and maternal cousin to Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan. The family has maintained close ties to the Al Nahyan ruling family through both political service and marriage alliances.
data.modernStatus: The Al Hamed family maintains connections to the UAE's ruling establishment through historical political service and marriage ties with the Al Nahyan family. Sheikh Ahmed bin Hamed al Hamed's legacy in establishing the UAE's media infrastructure continues to influence the country's information sector.

data.history: The Al Suroor family appears prominently in UAE governance and business, with Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan serving as the first Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from its founding in 1980 into the mid-1990s. During this period, Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman under Sheikh Suroor, making Al Suroor a key figure in the UAE's early economic development and monetary policy establishment. The family name also appears in contemporary contexts, with Shahin Suroor being noted as an Emirati footballer born in 1996, and Sheikha Fatima bint Suroor Al Nahyan marrying into the ruling Al Nahyan family as the wife of Sheikh Khaled bin Mohamed Al Nahyan, the current Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi. The research indicates connections to both the central banking sector and the ruling establishment of the UAE, though the specific origins and early history of the Al Suroor family are not detailed in the available sources.

data.history: The Al Manaseer family appears to be a prominent business family in Jordan with significant operations across the Middle East. The most documented figure is Ziyad Manasir, born in Amman, Jordan in 1965, who founded the Manaseer Group in 1999. Initially starting with a chemical and fertilizer distribution company with 15 employees, the group has expanded to include 16 subsidiary companies operating in infrastructure, energy, business solutions, consumer products, and chemicals. The family name appears in various locations across the Gulf, including Qatar where Fereej Al Manaseer is a district in Al Rayyan Municipality, suggesting historical presence or connections in the region. In Jordan, the Al Manaseer clan is listed as one of the branches of the larger Abbadi tribe, specifically under the Al-Jarūmiyyah grouping, indicating tribal affiliations within Jordan's traditional social structure.
data.description: The Al Manaseer is a prominent business family from Jordan, best known through Ziyad Manasir who built a major industrial and commercial empire. They have tribal connections within Jordan's Abbadi tribe structure.
data.modernStatus: Major industrial and commercial conglomerate through Manaseer Group, with 16 subsidiary companies operating primarily in Jordan and the West Bank, involved in construction, energy, chemicals, and various business sectors

data.history: The Al Sudairy family is a powerful tribal family from the Najd region of Saudi Arabia, belonging to the noble Dawasir tribe. The family's political prominence began in the early 20th century when Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi (1869-1936) became a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and one of the early supporters of King Abdulaziz during his conquest of Saudi Arabia. Following the formation of the Saudi state, Ahmed bin Mohammed served as governor in multiple provinces including Washm, Sudair, Qassim, and Aflaj, while his sons were appointed as governors across various regions. The Al Sudairy family's most significant contribution to Saudi history came through Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi's marriage to King Abdulaziz. Born in Al Ghat in 1900, Hussa married King Abdulaziz twice - first in 1913 when she was thirteen, then again in 1919 after a brief divorce and intermediate marriage to the king's half-brother. This second marriage produced seven sons known as the 'Sudairi Seven' and four daughters, making her the most valued spouse of King Abdulaziz as she bore him more sons than any other wife. The Sudairi Seven became the most powerful faction within the Saudi royal family, with their influence growing dramatically after Prince Fahd's appointment as Crown Prince in 1975 and King in 1982. The brothers systematically consolidated power by controlling key government positions - Prince Sultan as Defense Minister, Prince Nayef as Interior Minister, and Prince Salman as Riyadh Governor. Two of the seven (Fahd and Salman) became kings of Saudi Arabia, while two others (Sultan and Nayef) served as Crown Princes.
data.description: The Al Sudairy are a powerful noble family from the Dawasir tribe in Najd, Saudi Arabia, who became the most influential faction within the Saudi royal family through their matriarchal connection to King Abdulaziz via Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi, mother of the 'Sudairi Seven' including two future kings.
data.originStory: The Al Sudairy family originates from the town of Al Ghat in the Najd region and are part of the noble Dawasir tribe. Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi (1869-1936) was a powerful tribal chief who supported King Abdulaziz's conquest of Saudi Arabia in the early 20th century.
data.modernStatus: The Al Sudairy remain the most powerful faction within the Saudi royal family, with King Salman currently on the throne and Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman positioned as heir. The family maintains control over key government positions and business interests through the Sudairi Seven's descendants.

data.history: The Dawasir (Arabic: الدواسر) are an Arab tribe with their main base in southern Najd, specifically in Wadi Al-Dawasir in Saudi Arabia. The tribe originates from a confederation of tribes of the Azd and the Banu Taghlib (also known as Taghlib ibn Wa'il), with the 'original' Dawasir believed to be descendants from Doser (military settlement) in Najd. The Dawasir tribe has historically maintained close ties with the Saudi royal family, playing key roles in supporting the early Saudi state militarily and politically. These connections were solidified through intermarriage, most notably between King AbdulAziz Al Saud and Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi of the Sudairi family, a respected sub-clan of the Dawasir. She bore seven sons, famously known as the Sudairi Seven, including Kings Fahd and Salman. The cities of Dammam and Khobar in Eastern Saudi Arabia were founded in 1923 by the Dawasir tribe that migrated from Bahrain after King AbdulAziz allowed them to settle within the area. In Bahrain, the Dawasir were described as the 'second largest and most powerful tribe after the Utub,' so powerful that they recognized Sheikh 'Isa Al Khalifa as ruler in name only and considered themselves immune from taxation. Virtually all members of the tribe left Bahrain for Dammam, Saudi Arabia in 1923 after opposing the British overthrow of Sheikh Isa ibn Ali Al Khalifa, suspecting that the new ruler would attempt to tighten control over them with British support.
data.description: The Dawasir are among the most powerful and influential Arab tribes of Arabia, numbering over 6 million, with their main base in southern Najd's Wadi Al-Dawasir. They are the maternal uncles of much of the House of Saud family through strategic intermarriages.
data.originStory: The Dawasir originates from a confederation of tribes of the Azd and the Banu Taghlib (also known as Taghlib ibn Wa'il). The 'original' Dawasir are believed to be descendants from Doser (military settlement) in Najd, Saudi Arabia, with Wadi ad-Dawasir becoming their established homeland.
data.modernStatus: The Dawasir remain one of the largest Arab tribes with significant political influence in Saudi Arabia through their connection to the ruling Al Saud family. Notable modern figures include Yasir Al-Rumayyan (chairman of the Public Investment Fund), Fahad Al-Jalajel (Saudi Minister of Health), and Walid al-Samaani (Saudi Minister of Justice).
data.nameEtymology: There are various theories surrounding the definition and origin of the term 'Dawasir'. The two most popular theories are that it was derived from either the name of the tribe's purported forebear Dosser or the eponymous Arabic word which translates to 'soldiers'. Other sources include terms such as the Arabic word for Lion or a type of Arabian horse.

data.history: The Al Hefeiti family is an Emirati family with documented presence in the UAE, though specific historical origins are not detailed in available sources. The family has achieved notable prominence in contemporary Emirati diplomacy and government service. The most prominent known member is Amiera Obaid AlHefeiti, who has built a distinguished career in diplomatic service starting from 2011 when she joined the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and International Cooperation. Her career trajectory demonstrates the family's integration into the UAE's modern governmental and diplomatic institutions, rising through various positions at the United Nations and ultimately being appointed as UAE Ambassador to the Netherlands in 2025.
data.description: An Emirati family known primarily through diplomat Amiera Obaid AlHefeiti, who serves as UAE Ambassador to the Netherlands and has held senior positions in the UAE's diplomatic corps.
data.modernStatus: The family is represented in UAE's diplomatic service, with Amiera AlHefeiti serving as UAE Ambassador to the Kingdom of the Netherlands and Permanent Representative to the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons since 2025.

data.history: The research provided does not contain any direct information about the Al Bu Mahasin family. While the Wikipedia article on Abd al-Rahman al-Fasi mentions a work titled 'Ibtihaj al-qulub bi khabar al-Shaykh Abi al-Mahasin wa wa shaykhihi al-Majdhub' (a biography of Abu l-Mahasin Yusuf al-Fasi and other Moroccan sheikhs), this refers to a Moroccan sheikh named Abu l-Mahasin Yusuf al-Fasi, not the GCC Al Bu Mahasin family. The Al-Ali tribe article from Iraq mentions Muhammad Hasan Abi al-Mahasin as a poet and politician who was a leader of the Iraqi revolution against British occupation and later became Minister of Education, but this appears to be an Iraqi figure with the title 'Abi al-Mahasin' rather than a member of a Gulf Al Bu Mahasin family. The Al-Maghili article contains no references to any Al Bu Mahasin family. Without specific research about the GCC Al Bu Mahasin family, no verified historical narrative can be constructed.
data.description: No verified information about the Al Bu Mahasin family is available in the provided research materials.

data.history: Al Jbour is a family from the Bani Sakhr tribe, one of the most prominent Bedouin tribes in Jordan. The family is specifically mentioned among the families from Bani Sakhr residing in the Al-Muwaqqar district in the Amman Governorate of north-western Jordan, alongside other Bani Sakhr families including Al-Khraisha, Al-Arabid, and Al-Qudahh. The Al-Muwaqqar area, which contains the ruins of an Umayyad palace (Qasr al-Muwaqqar), has historical significance as it was located on the ancient caravan route between Amman and Azraq via Qusayr 'Amra, where these localities served as way stations. The area gained particular prominence during the early 20th century when Emir Abdullah, later King Abdullah I of Jordan, moved his camp to Al-Muwaqqar in August 1922, near the desert castles in the Khraishas' traditional territory, where he organized Bedouin forces to defend against Wahhabi Ikhwan attacks. The connection to Al-Muwaqqar and the broader Bani Sakhr tribal structure indicates the Al Jbour family's integration into the traditional Bedouin social and territorial organization of the region.
data.description: Al Jbour is a family from the Bani Sakhr tribe residing in the Al-Muwaqqar district of Jordan's Amman Governorate. They are part of the broader Bani Sakhr tribal confederation, one of Jordan's most influential Bedouin tribes.

connections.context: Al Jbour is listed among the Bani Sakhr families residing in Al-Muwaqqar district
Both Al Jbour and Al-Khraisha are mentioned as Bani Sakhr families in the same region of Al-Muwaqqar

data.history:
The House of Al-Sabah (آل صباح) is the ruling family of Kuwait, originating from the Bani Utbah confederation. According to oral traditions, the Al Sabah family were expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to their predatory habits of preying on caravans in Basra and trading ships in the Shatt al-Arab. Prior to settling in Kuwait, they settled across various regions in southern Iran and Iraq. One tradition states they fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south, then returned and migrated with other families to Zubara on Qatar's west coast before finally settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s, where finding water, they established their settlement.

Soon after founding the settlement in Kuwait, a Sabah became leader, ruling until his death in 1762. The reign of Mubarak the Great (1896–1915) forged the critical alliance between Kuwait and Britain. In 1896, Mubarak killed his brothers Mohammed and Jarrah and announced he ruled in their stead. Sheikh Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah ended the British protectorate status by signing a treaty with Britain on 19 June 1961, introduced Kuwait's Constitution in 1962, and established Parliament in 1963.

During the Gulf War, Emir Sheikh Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah ran an exiled government from Ta'if, Saudi Arabia, directing underground resistance and providing services to Kuwaitis who remained. The family has maintained continuous rule through 17 rulers, from Sheikh Sabah I bin Jaber (1752–1762) to the current Emir Sheikh Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (2023–present). The family alternated leadership between Al-Jaber and Al-Salem branches traditionally, though this changed when Nawaf Al-Ahmad succeeded his half-brother Sabah Al-Ahmad in 2020.
data.description: The ruling family of Kuwait since the mid-1700s, originating from the Bani Utbah confederation and establishing themselves after settling in Kuwait around 1750.
data.originStory: According to oral tradition told to the Political Agent by Shaikh Abdulla, the Sabahs fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south finding conditions bleaker, returned and with other families migrated to Zubara on Qatar's west coast, then finding conditions no better migrated north to Kuwait where finding water they settled. Another tradition states they were expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to predatory habits before settling across southern Iran and Iraq, then finally Kuwait.
data.modernStatus: Currently rules Kuwait as absolute monarchy, with Sheikh Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah serving as Emir since 2023. Family members hold key government positions including defense, interior, and foreign affairs ministries. Has faced recent corruption scandals involving hundreds of millions in embezzled funds.

folkLegends.title: Origin of Bani Utub Name
folkLegends.story: On the last leg of their journey to Kuwait, they had 'atabu-ila al-shimal' (moved to the north), and according to one tradition, this was the origin of the name Bani Utub.

data.nameEtymology: Al-Sabah means 'the morning' in Arabic

migrationPath.description: Fled drought in central Arabia, migrated south then to Zubara

data.history: The Al Nayef family represents a branch of the House of Saud, descended from Prince Nayef bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1934-2012), who was one of the Sudairi Seven - the seven full brothers born to King Abdulaziz and Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi. Prince Nayef served as Minister of Interior from 1975 until his death in 2012, becoming one of the most powerful figures in the Saudi security apparatus. He was appointed Crown Prince in October 2011 but died in Geneva in June 2012 before ascending to the throne. His son Muhammad bin Nayef continued the family's prominence in security affairs, serving as Minister of Interior from 2012-2017 and Crown Prince from 2015-2017, making them the first father-son duo in Saudi history to both serve as crown prince. However, Muhammad bin Nayef was removed from all positions in June 2017 and has been detained since March 2020. The family's influence peaked during the reigns of Kings Fahd, Abdullah, and early years of King Salman, when they controlled Saudi Arabia's vast internal security apparatus and counterterrorism operations.
data.description: A prominent branch of the House of Saud descended from Prince Nayef bin Abdulaziz, known for their control of Saudi Arabia's interior ministry and security apparatus. The family produced two crown princes but lost power in 2017.
data.originStory: The Al Nayef family originates from the House of Saud, specifically through Prince Nayef bin Abdulaziz Al Saud, who was born in Ta'if in 1934 to King Abdulaziz and Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi. As one of the Sudairi Seven, Nayef belonged to the most influential faction within the royal family.
data.modernStatus: The family's political influence has significantly diminished since 2017. Muhammad bin Nayef has been detained since March 2020 on treason charges, while his brother Saud bin Nayef serves as Governor of Eastern Province. The family has been stripped of much of their wealth and security portfolios.

data.history: The Awamir (Arabic: العوامر) is a Bedouin Arab tribe in the United Arab Emirates and Oman, closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. Originally from the steppes north of the Hadhramut, they settled in the northern Arabian Peninsula through a migration process that took place over 500 years. The tribe roamed across the entire Oman peninsula, from Muscat and Nizwa to Abu Dhabi and Liwa, and were characterized as warlike and fiercely independent camel breeders and raiders before settling in the 1960s. The Awamir in the Abu Dhabi area were considered affiliated to the Bani Yas and frequently supported them in conflicts, including Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan's decisive 1848 victory against the Wahhabis at Buraimi and Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan's extended war with Qatar in the 1880s. By the 1930s, the decline of pearling led to increased raiding activities, culminating in open conflicts and the 1946 war between Dubai and Abu Dhabi. A significant tribal schism occurred in the 1940s when headman Salim bin Hamad bin Rakkad led part of the tribe to Al Hasa in 1943, with this portion eventually becoming Saudi citizens, while the bulk remained at Buraimi under Al Bu Falah protection. By 1968, some 1,721 members were identified in a census, many having taken employment with oil companies as the tribe transitioned from nomadic life to settlement.
data.description: The Awamir is a Bedouin Arab tribe in the UAE and Oman, historically known as warlike camel breeders and raiders who were closely affiliated with the Bani Yas confederation. They originated from the steppes north of the Hadhramut and settled across the northern Arabian Peninsula over a 500-year migration period.
data.originStory: The Awamir originated in the steppes to the north of the Hadhramut, settling in the north through a migration process that took place over 500 years.
data.modernStatus: By 1968, the tribe had largely transitioned from nomadic life to settlement, with many members taking employment with oil companies. The tribe experienced a permanent division, with part becoming Saudi citizens while the majority remained in the UAE-Oman region.

data.history: The Al Shaalan family is a prominent tribal family that spans Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and southern Iraq, tracing their lineage to the Ruwala tribe, which is part of the larger Anizah confederation. The family rose to prominence as the traditional rulers and sheikhs of the Ruwala tribe, with their influence extending across the northern Arabian steppes and into the Levant. Prince Nuri Al Shaalan served as the emir of the Ruwala tribe in the early 20th century, establishing the family's political prominence in the region. The family has produced notable military leaders, politicians, and diplomats across multiple countries. In Iraq, Hazim al-Shaalan served as Defense Minister from 2004-2005 under the Iraqi Interim Government, though he later fled the country amid corruption allegations. In Saudi Arabia, Muhammad Al Shaalan commanded the Royal Saudi Air Force from 2014 until his death in 2015 during the Yemen intervention. The family has also produced influential diplomatic figures like Nayef bin Fawwaz Al Shaalan, and has maintained marriage connections with the Saudi royal family through figures like Hessa bint Trad Al Shaalan, who was a wife of King Abdullah.
data.description: The Al Shaalan are a prominent tribal family from the Ruwala tribe spanning Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and southern Iraq, traditionally serving as sheikhs of their tribe with members holding high military and political positions across the region.
data.originStory: The Al Shaalan trace their origins to the Ruwala tribe, which is a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation. The Ruwala became so large and powerful that they practically developed into an independent tribe, centered around Al-Jouf and the valley of Wadi Sirhan and extending into Jordan and Syria.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant political and social influence across the Gulf region, with members serving in high-level military, diplomatic, and government positions. They have established marriage connections with the Saudi royal family and continue to hold traditional leadership roles within their tribal structure.

data.history: The Al Mehairbi (Maharibah/المحاربة/المحيربي) are identified as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. According to early 20th century British sources, the Maharibah were considered to have originated from the Na'im tribe before their incorporation into the Bani Yas confederation. The Bani Yas as a whole originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia and settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700, with various branches later dispersing to other locations including Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Al Ain, and Buraimi. The confederation was led by the Al Bu Falah (from which the Al Nahyan ruling family descends) and included approximately 20 tribes across the region from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to southeastern Qatar. By the early 20th century, J.C. Lorimer estimated the total Bani Yas population at around 12,000, though this declined significantly through the mid-20th century due to emigration for employment opportunities in other Gulf states, before recovering after the accession of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and the UAE's oil-driven development.
data.description: Al Mehairbi (Maharibah) is a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the UAE, originally believed to have Na'im tribal origins before incorporation into the confederation. They are part of the broader tribal structure that includes the ruling families of Abu Dhabi and Dubai.
data.originStory: According to 19th and early 20th century sources, the Maharibah were considered to have originated from the Na'im tribe before their incorporation into the Bani Yas confederation.
data.tribalOrigin: Na'im (according to historical sources, before incorporation into Bani Yas)
data.modernStatus: One of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas confederation in the modern UAE, with members holding various positions in Emirati society. Basheer Al Mehairbi served as Director General of the Abu Dhabi Housing Society as of 2020.

migrationPath.description: Settlement of Bani Yas confederation including Maharibah branch in Liwa oasis

data.history: The Al Brahui are an ethnolinguistic group of pastoralists principally found in the Balochistan region of Pakistan, and to a smaller extent in Afghanistan and Iran. They speak Brahui, which belongs to the Dravidian language family and is geographically isolated from other Dravidian languages by over 1,500 kilometers. The most common theory suggests the Brahui were part of a Dravidian migration into northwestern Pakistan in the 3rd millennium BC, remaining in Sarawan and Jahlawan since before 2000 BC, though some scholars argue they migrated from central India only after 1000 AD. Genetic evidence shows the Brahui population is indistinguishable from neighboring Balochi speakers and genetically distant from central Dravidian speakers, supporting the relict hypothesis rather than recent migration. Historically nomadic, the Brahui formed the Khanate of Kalat in the 1660s under Ahmad Khan I as a response to Mughal penetration into their traditional lands. The khanate reached its zenith under Nasir Khan I in the late 18th century before accepting British protectorate status and eventually being absorbed into Pakistan after Partition. Expatriate Brahui communities exist in the Gulf states including Qatar and the UAE, though specific migration details to these regions are not documented in available sources.
data.description: An ethnolinguistic group of Dravidian-speaking pastoralists primarily from Balochistan, Pakistan, with smaller communities in Afghanistan, Iran, and expatriate populations in Gulf states including Qatar and UAE.
data.originStory: The origins remain unclear with two competing theories: either they are remnants of ancient Dravidian populations that migrated to northwestern Pakistan in the 3rd millennium BC, or they migrated from central India sometime after 1000 AD. Genetic evidence supports the former theory.
data.modernStatus: Most Brahui have settled in Pakistan's Balochistan province with smaller populations in neighboring countries. According to Elfenbein's estimates, only about 15% of Brahui tribesmen are primary speakers of the Brahui language, with many having adopted Balochi as their primary language. The 2023 Pakistan Census enumerated 2.78 million Brahui-speakers across Pakistan.

data.nameEtymology: The origin of the word 'Brahui' is uncertain. Two main hypotheses exist: Mikhail Andronov suggested derivation from Dravidian meaning 'Northern hillmen,' while Josef Elfenbein proposed derivation from Saraiki brāhō, referring to prophet Abraham, possibly distinguishing neo-Muslim nomadic pastoralists.

data.history: The Al Bizenjo (Brahui: بزنجو) is an ethnic Brahui tribe inhabiting Balochistan province in Pakistan, belonging to the Jhalawani branch of the Brahui tribes. According to the official list by Mir Ahmad Yar, the last Khan of Kalat, Bizenjo was originally one of the Jatt tribes inhabiting the region alongside Zehri and Mengal. Though the Bizenjo in eastern Makran are Brahui by origin, they have been Baluchified in language and customs due to prolonged residence in Makran. The family gained significant political prominence through Ghaus Bakhsh Bizenjo, who became a key founding member of the National Awami Party and served as the 3rd Governor of Balochistan from 1972 to 1973, playing a crucial role as a signatory to Pakistan's 1973 constitution. The family has maintained its political influence through subsequent generations, with descendants like Hasil Bizenjo continuing to lead the National Party, which traces its legacy directly from the Kalat State National Party founded by Ghaus Bakhsh Bizenjo, Mir Gul Khan Nasir, and Mir Abdul Aziz Kurd.
data.description: The Al Bizenjo is a Brahui tribe from Balochistan, Pakistan, that has produced prominent political leaders including Governor Ghaus Bakhsh Bizenjo. The family belongs to the Jhalawani branch of Brahui tribes and has been influential in Baloch nationalist politics.
data.originStory: According to Mir Ahmad Yar, the last Khan of Kalat, Bizenjo was originally one of the Jatt tribes inhabiting the Balochistan region. The Bizenjo in eastern Makran, though Brahui by origin, became Baluchified in language and customs due to prolonged residence in Makran.
data.modernStatus: The family remains politically active through the National Party in Pakistan, with members holding leadership positions in Balochistan provincial politics. They continue to advocate for Baloch rights and democratic governance in Pakistan.

data.history: The Al Khetran (also spelled Khetran) are an ethnic group native to the Barkhan region of Balochistan, Pakistan. According to Wikipedia's ethnic groups data, the Khetran population is estimated at 150,000 people, with at least 100,000 of whom speak the Khetrani language as of 2017. They are classified among the smaller ethnic groups in Pakistan that have a population of at least 100,000 but were not enumerated separately in the 2023 census. The Khetran appear to be indigenous to their current territory in Balochistan, though specific historical details about their origins, migrations, or political evolution are not provided in the available research. During British colonial rule, the tribal areas of Marri, Bugti, Khetran and Chaghi were brought under the direct administration of a British Agent, eventually becoming part of the Chief Commissioner's Province of Balochistan.
data.description: The Al Khetran are an ethnic group of approximately 150,000 people native to the Barkhan region of Balochistan, Pakistan, with their own distinct Khetrani language spoken by at least 100,000 members.
data.modernStatus: The Khetran remain in their traditional territory in the Barkhan region of Balochistan and maintain their distinct linguistic identity with the Khetrani language, though they were not separately enumerated in Pakistan's 2023 census.

data.history: The Al Talpur (Talpur) family is a Baloch dynasty that originated from the Talpur tribe and rose to prominence in Sindh during the late 18th century. According to the research, Mir Muhammad Nasir Khan Talpur's great-grandfather, Mir Bahram Khan, was the Vizier of Ghulam Shah Kalhoro, indicating the family's earlier service to the Kalhoro rulers before establishing their own dynasty. The Talpur ruled Sindh from 1783 to 1843, with Mir Fateh Ali Khan being the first Mir of Hyderabad. The dynasty reached its zenith under various rulers who made Hyderabad their capital and constructed significant fortifications including the Pakka Qilla (Brick Fort) and Kacha Qilla (Mud Fort). The last independent ruler was Mir Muhammad Nasir Khan Talpur (1804-1845), who became the sixth Mir of Hyderabad in 1839. His reign ended with the British conquest of Sindh following decisive defeats at the Battle of Miani on February 17, 1843, and the Battle of Hyderabad on March 24, 1843, after which Sindh was annexed to British India. The research indicates that Talpur was specifically identified as 'a Baloch dynasty that originated in the modern-day Sindh region of Pakistan' and that 'The Talpur Baloch were a prominent Baloch tribe that rose to power in Sindh during the late 18th century.'
data.description: Al Talpur is a Baloch dynasty that ruled Sindh from 1783 to 1843, with their last ruler Mir Muhammad Nasir Khan Talpur being defeated by the British East India Company in 1843. The family established Hyderabad as their capital and are remembered as the final independent rulers of Sindh.
data.originStory: The Al Talpur family descended from the Baloch Talpur tribe. Through Mir Muhammad Nasir Khan Talpur's mother, who was from Shoran in Kalat and daughter of Sher Muhammad Khan Rind, the family also claims direct descent from the legendary Baloch mercenary Mir Chakar Khan Rind. The family's rise to power came after serving the Kalhoro rulers, with Mir Bahram Khan serving as Vizier to Ghulam Shah Kalhoro before the Talpur established their own dynasty.
data.modernStatus: The Al Talpur legacy is preserved through historical sites including the Tombs of the Talpur Mirs (Cubbās) at Hirabad, Hyderabad, which remain important cultural and historical landmarks of Sindh. The family's architectural contributions include the Badshahi Bungalow at Latifabad, Hyderabad, commissioned in 1863 by Mir Hassan Ali Khan Talpur, which today serves as the Talpur mansion.

data.history: The Al Mahmood family encompasses multiple distinct lineages across different regions and contexts. The most documented branch appears to be the Al Mahmoud of Qatar and UAE, who are described as a Sunni Arab family from the Banu Tamim tribe, specifically from Al Maʿaḍīd, with historical connections to the Al Thani ruling family. They migrated from Najd, specifically from Hotat Bani Tamim. However, the research also reveals other notable Al Mahmood individuals from completely different backgrounds, including Pakistani nuclear scientist Sultan Bashiruddin Mahmood (born 1940 in Amritsar to a Punjabi Muslim family), and British politician Shabana Mahmood (born 1980 in Birmingham to Pakistani parents from Mirpur, Azad Kashmir). These represent separate family lines that happen to share the surname Mahmood/Mahmoud, rather than a single cohesive family lineage.
data.description: Multiple distinct family lineages sharing the Al Mahmood/Mahmoud surname, including Gulf Arab families from Banu Tamim origins in Qatar/UAE, and South Asian families with Pakistani/Kashmiri roots.
data.originStory: The Qatar/UAE branch originates from the Banu Tamim tribe in Najd, specifically from Hotat Bani Tamim, with historical migration to the Gulf region. Other documented Al Mahmood families have separate origins in the Indian subcontinent.
data.modernStatus: The family name appears across multiple sectors: Gulf business and political networks (Qatar/UAE branch), nuclear science and academia (Pakistani branch), and British politics (Shabana Mahmood as UK Home Secretary).

migrationPath.description: Migration of Al Mahmoud branch from Najd to Gulf region

data.history: The Al Sorayai family's documented history begins with their business ventures in the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. In 1953, Abdullah and Nasser Abdul Aziz Al-Sorayai established 'Abdullah and Nasser Abdul Aziz Al-Sorayai Partnership Company' in Mecca, marking the beginning of what would become one of the largest carpet and textile manufacturing operations in the Middle East. The company expanded significantly over the decades, establishing Al-Sorayai Carpet Factory in 1985 and Jeddah Synthetic Yarns Factory in 1989. Through a series of corporate restructurings and mergers in 2007, the various family enterprises were consolidated into Al-Sorayai Commercial Industrial Group. The family's business reached public prominence when they converted their company to a public joint stock company in 2010, listing on the Saudi Stock Exchange (Tadawul). In 2020, the company was renamed to Naseej Global Trading Company, representing one of the world's top 15 carpet and textile manufacturers.
data.description: The Al Sorayai family are Saudi business entrepreneurs who built one of the Middle East's leading carpet and textile manufacturing empires, starting from a partnership in Mecca in 1953.
data.modernStatus: Major industrial family controlling Naseej Global Trading Company (formerly Al-Sorayai Commercial Industrial Group), one of the world's top 15 carpet manufacturers and the Middle East's leader in carpet, textile and synthetic yarn production, publicly traded on Saudi Stock Exchange since 2010

data.history:
The Al Muqbel family appears in historical records through their connection to the Al Sheikh family in the Riyadh region during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Haya bint Abdul Rahman Al Muqbel was the mother of Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh, who became the third wife of King Abdulaziz (founder of Saudi Arabia) in 1902 immediately after his capture of Riyadh. Through this marriage, Al Muqbel became connected to the Saudi royal family as maternal ancestors of King Faisal, who was born in 1906 to Tarfa bint Abdullah.

The family's most significant historical role was through Haya bint Abdul Rahman Al Muqbel, who served as one of the maternal grandparents who raised young Prince Faisal after his mother Tarfa died when he was only six months old. Under the mentorship of his maternal grandparents, including Haya bint Abdul Rahman Al Muqbel, Prince Faisal completed his studies of reading the Qur'an and studying Islamic law and doctrines by age nine. This early education would prove crucial as Faisal later became one of Saudi Arabia's most influential kings from 1964 to 1975.

The Al Muqbel family's legacy is thus intertwined with Saudi royal history through their role in raising King Faisal during his formative years. Their village location near Riyadh positioned them within the central Nejd region's social and political networks during the critical period of Saudi state formation in the early 20th century.
data.description: A family from the Riyadh region whose most notable member was Haya bint Abdul Rahman Al Muqbel, maternal grandmother and guardian of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia.
data.modernStatus: Historical records show their connection to the Saudi royal family through King Faisal's maternal lineage, though current family status is not documented in available sources.

data.history: Al Shahrani (Al-Mushayt branch) represents a family from the Al-Ghanem branch of the Shahran tribe in Saudi Arabia, recognized as sheikhs and leaders of the broader Shahran tribal confederation. The Al-Mushayt lineage traces back to Mishait bin Salem bin Hussein bin Ibrahim bin Saad bin Muslih bin Ali Al-Ghanoumi Al-Rashidi Al-Shahrani, establishing their connection to the Al-Rashid lineage within the Shahran tribal structure. The family has maintained prominence in the Khamis Mushait region and Wadi Bisha, where they are often referred to as 'Bisha Mushayt,' indicating their geographic distribution across these key areas of the Asir region. One notable connection to the broader Al Shahrani network appears through marriage alliances with the Saudi royal family, as evidenced by Fatima bint Abdulaziz bin Mushait Al Shahrani, who was married to King Faisal bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and was the mother of Princess Munira. This marriage connection illustrates the family's integration into the highest levels of Saudi society during the mid-20th century. The family's status as tribal leaders and their geographic concentration in the Asir region positions them within the complex tribal and political networks that have shaped southwestern Saudi Arabia.
data.description: Al Shahrani (Al-Mushayt branch) is a prominent family from the Al-Ghanem branch of the Shahran tribe, serving as recognized sheikhs and leaders in the Khamis Mushait and Wadi Bisha regions of southwestern Saudi Arabia. The family has maintained significant social standing, including marriage connections to the Saudi royal family.
data.originStory: According to documented lineage records, the Al-Mushayt family descends from Mishait bin Salem bin Hussein bin Ibrahim bin Saad bin Muslih bin Ali Al-Ghanoumi Al-Rashidi Al-Shahrani. Their origins trace to the Al-Rashid lineage, described as descendants of Ajram bin Nahis bin Afras bin Half bin Aftal, who is identified as Khath'am bin Anmar, indicating ancient Arabian tribal roots in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains traditional tribal leadership roles in the Asir region, particularly in Khamis Mushait and Wadi Bisha areas. Their historical integration into Saudi elite circles through royal marriage connections suggests continued social prominence, though specific contemporary business or political activities are not documented in available sources.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Mushayt derives from the ancestor Mishait bin Salem, with the family name preserving this patrilineal connection through traditional Arabian naming conventions.

data.history: The Al Braikan family came to international attention through a significant insider trading case in 2009. According to U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission allegations, Kuwaiti trader Hazem Al-Braikan engaged in insider trading after misleading the public about possible takeover bids for two companies. The case concluded tragically when Al-Braikan was found dead of a gunshot wound to the head in his home in Kuwait City on July 26, 2009, just three days after being sued by the SEC. Kuwaiti police ruled his death a suicide. The SEC subsequently reached a $6.5 million settlement of civil insider trading charges with his estate and others, marking one of the notable international insider trading cases involving Gulf nationals.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family that gained international attention through a high-profile 2009 insider trading case involving Hazem Al-Braikan and the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.
data.modernStatus: Known primarily through the 2009 SEC insider trading case involving Hazem Al-Braikan, which resulted in a $6.5 million settlement

data.history: The Al Wohaibi family appears in the historical record through archaeological and governmental contexts. Dr. Fahad al Wohaibi served as director of the National Museum of Kuwait and made significant archaeological discoveries, including fragments of vessels dated to the Ubaid period at the H3 site in the Subiya region of northern Kuwait. His archaeological work was part of broader international research efforts in Kuwait's Subiya region, which revealed important Bronze Age settlements and burial sites dating from 2500-1500 BC. A separate branch of the family is represented by Khalfan bin Nasser Al Wahaibi from Oman, who was born in Muscat in 1940 and pursued careers in both government service and banking. He served in various ministerial positions including Minister of Social Affairs and Labour (1972-1979) and Minister of Electricity and Water (1981-1990), while also holding leadership roles in major Omani financial institutions including the National Bank of Oman from 1992-2004.
data.description: The Al Wohaibi family includes notable figures in both Kuwaiti archaeology and Omani government and banking, with documented contributions to cultural preservation and public service in the Gulf region.
data.modernStatus: Family members have held prominent positions in cultural institutions and government, including museum directorship in Kuwait and ministerial roles in Oman, as well as leadership positions in major Gulf banking institutions.

data.history: The Khashoggi family has Turkish origins, with their ancestors making the Hajj pilgrimage from Kayseri to Mecca approximately four centuries ago and deciding to remain in the Arabian Peninsula. The family surname 'Khashoggi' means 'spoon maker' (kaşıkçı) in Turkish, reflecting their ancestral profession. Muhammad Khashoggi, the family patriarch, served as King Abdul Aziz Al Saud's personal doctor, establishing the family's prominence in Saudi Arabia. His son Adnan Khashoggi (1935-2017) became one of the world's most famous arms dealers and businessmen, amassing a fortune of $4 billion at his peak in the early 1980s through his role as intermediary between Western defense companies and the Saudi government. The family achieved international prominence through Adnan's extravagant lifestyle and business empire, but also faced tragedy with the 2018 assassination of journalist Jamal Khashoggi, Adnan's nephew, at the Saudi consulate in Istanbul.
data.description: A prominent Saudi family of Turkish origin whose patriarch served as King Abdul Aziz's personal doctor, later achieving international fame through arms dealer Adnan Khashoggi and tragic notoriety through journalist Jamal Khashoggi's assassination.
data.originStory: The Khashoggi ancestors were Turkish citizens from Kayseri who made the Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca some four centuries earlier and decided to stay in the Arabian Peninsula rather than return to Turkey.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant business interests through various descendants, though their prominence has been overshadowed by the international controversy surrounding Jamal Khashoggi's 2018 assassination by Saudi agents.
data.nameEtymology: The surname 'Khashoggi' derives from the Turkish word 'kaşıkçı' meaning 'spoon maker', indicating the family's ancestral profession in Turkey.

data.history: The Kurdi family referenced in the research appears to be a Syrian Kurdish family from Kobanî, Syria. The family's story became internationally known through the tragic death of two-year-old Alan Kurdi (born Alan Shenu) in September 2015. According to the research, the family name was actually Shenu, with 'Kurdi' being used in Turkey due to their Kurdish ethnic background. The family had moved between various cities in northern Syria to escape the Syrian civil war and ISIL attacks, eventually settling in Turkey before attempting the fatal Mediterranean crossing. Alan Kurdi's aunt Tima Kurdi had filed for refugee sponsorship to bring family members to Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, though these applications faced various bureaucratic challenges. The family's tragedy became a symbol of the broader Syrian refugee crisis and had significant political impact, particularly in the 2015 Canadian federal election.
data.description: A Syrian Kurdish refugee family originally from Kobanî, Syria, whose tragic story during the 2015 refugee crisis brought international attention to the Syrian civil war's human cost.
